title: Deep Learning meets Nonparametric Regression: Are Weight-Decayed DNNs Locally Adaptive?

reviews.summary_of_the_paper:
This paper examines training a specific neural network (namely parallel neural net) using weight decay and shows that the error rate matches the minimax bound for a specific class of functions (i.e., functions with bounded variation). 

It looks like a quite interesting effort and the result also appears to be significant. But I worry the paper is not presented at the level for general audience in ICLR. For example, I have a few questions/confusions after reading the submission: 

1. What exactly is weight decay? I actually could not find its definition/elaboration except for eq. 2, which is just L2 regularization? I was guessing it could relate to learning rate, momentum, some dynamic shrinkage on the fly...
2. Do we assume that Eq.2 can be optimally solved? It is a minimax result related to sample complexity so I believe the authors assume that Eq. can be optimally solved --- I think sometimes ignoring computational tractability is fine (and perhaps had been a standard practice in statistics) but it would be helpful to bring this upfront. 
3. What's so special about parallel NN? I think most audience wants to understand at a higher level where the magic happens and why the magic wont happen for the standard MLP, e.g., only limit of existing theoretical tools or parallel NN possesses some special structure? 
----
Thank you for addressing the presentation concern. I have raised my scores to 6 -- do not feel am an expert in this area so no confidence to move to 7.

Weakness: there is room for improvement in presentation.

It looks like a quite interesting effort and the result also appears to be significant. But I worry the paper is not presented at the level for general audience in ICLR. For example, I have a few questions/confusions after reading the submission: 

1. What exactly is weight decay? I actually could not find its definition/elaboration except for eq. 2, which is just L2 regularization? I was guessing it could relate to learning rate, momentum, some dynamic shrinkage on the fly...
2. Do we assume that Eq.2 can be optimally solved? It is a minimax result related to sample complexity so I believe the authors assume that Eq. can be optimally solved --- I think sometimes ignoring computational tractability is fine (and perhaps had been a standard practice in statistics) but it would be helpful to bring this upfront. 
3. What's so special about parallel NN? I think most audience wants to understand at a higher level where the magic happens and why the magic wont happen for the standard MLP, e.g., only limit of existing theoretical tools or parallel NN possesses some special structure?

reviews.text:
## New Peer Review

**Overall Assessment:**
This paper presents a significant and interesting effort, demonstrating that weight-decayed parallel neural networks achieve minimax rates for functions with bounded variation. The substance of the work is considered to be above the bar for ICLR, particularly after initial presentation concerns were addressed in revisions.

**Strengths:**
The core contribution of this paper is the rigorous demonstration that a specific architecture—weight-decayed parallel neural networks—can achieve minimax optimal error rates for an important class of functions, specifically those with bounded variation. This finding is both novel and impactful for understanding the theoretical capabilities of neural networks. Furthermore, the technical methodology employed, particularly the techniques related to "hacking into" and handling covering numbers, appears to be significant and represents a notable advancement in the field. The overall technical depth and the importance of establishing these minimax bounds contribute strongly to the paper's merit, placing its substance above the acceptance threshold.

**Weaknesses:**
While the technical contributions are strong, the paper's presentation could benefit from further refinement. Initial concerns were raised regarding the accessibility of the paper for a general ICLR audience, and even after revisions, there remains perceived room for improvement in clarity. At times, some technical details were confusing, which could hinder a broader understanding of the work's nuances and implications. A primary critique revolved around ensuring the clarity and accessibility of the presentation, especially concerning foundational concepts and the unique aspects of the proposed architecture.

**Suggestions for Improvement:**
To enhance the paper's clarity and impact, I offer the following suggestions:
1.  **Elaborate on Weight Decay:** Provide a clearer and more comprehensive definition or elaboration of "weight decay" beyond simply equating it to L2 regularization (as referenced by Eq. 2 in the manuscript). This would help readers less familiar with the specific context understand its role more deeply.
2.  **Address Computational Tractability:** Bring upfront the assumption that Eq. 2 (the optimization problem) can be optimally solved. Addressing the computational tractability of this optimization problem explicitly would be beneficial, as it is a practical consideration for many readers.
3.  **Highlight Parallel NN Uniqueness:** Explain more thoroughly what makes parallel neural networks special and why standard Multi-Layer Perceptrons (MLPs) might not achieve the same theoretical results. Offering a higher-level understanding of this distinction would greatly benefit the audience in appreciating the architectural choices and their consequences.

**Conclusion:**
This paper makes a substantial theoretical contribution by establishing that weight-decayed parallel neural networks can achieve minimax optimal error rates for functions with bounded variation, supported by significant technical work involving covering numbers. While the presentation still has room for improvement regarding clarity and accessibility for a broad ICLR audience, the core contribution is strong and highly valuable. The identified weaknesses are primarily related to presentation rather than fundamental scientific soundness. Given the significance of the results, I recommend acceptance.

**Rating:** 6: marginally above the acceptance threshold

reviews.summary_of_the_paper: The paper analyzes parallel neural networks for nonparametric regression (including approximation error and regression error analysis). Suppose the regression function has nonhomogeneous regularities, e.g., Besov functions or functions of bounded variation. Parallel neural networks trained with weight decay achieves near optimal estimation, indicating the adaptability of parallel neural networks.
reviews.strength_and_weaknesses:
============= Strength =============

The paper is clearly written with both theoretical and empirical results. The theoretical results are correct and empirical results are supportive.

The adaptability of neural networks is studied in many recent works, including its adaptability to data geometry and adaptability to structures in function spaces. This paper considers parallel neural networks and uses weight decay for controlling the complexity of the network, which circumvents sparsity constraints in many existing network approximation results.

============= Weakness =============

There is no related work section. Some of papers share similar ideas to parallel neural networks, for example, "Approximation and Non-parametric Estimation of ResNet-type Convolutional Neural Networks" and "Besov Function Approximation and Binary Classification on Low-Dimensional Manifolds Using Convolutional Residual Networks" both rely on implementing block sparse feedforward networks. The resulting (residual) convolutional network does not need a sparsity constraint in training.

The obtained rate of estimation is slightly slower than the optimal rate, which is attainable (up to log factors) in many recent works on using ReLU neural networks for nonparametric regression.

From experiments, parallel neural networks seem to achieve comparable performance to neural networks and conventional methods.
reviews.clarity_quality_novelty_and_reproducibil:
Some of the technical contributions need highlighting. For example, the conversion of weight decay objective to the constraint form in Equation (5) can be highlighted as a contribution -- if it does not appear in Parhi & Nowak (2021a). Another example is the decomposition of \mathcal{F}_{\parallel} and \mathcal{F}_{\perp}.

A discussion on why parallel neural networks can only achieve the optimal rate in the asymptotic sense ($L \to \infty$) is helpful. Is it because the weight decay introduces additional bias?

In Theorem 1, it is good to state how to choose weight decay $\lambda$.

Lemma 6 is relative difficult to understand, mainly because the first sentence begins with an equation.

Overall, the paper is well written. There are some typos and typeset issues, for example, "errorand" --> "error and" above Section 4.2

reviews.full_review_text:
Summary Of The Paper:
The paper analyzes parallel neural networks for nonparametric regression (including approximation error and regression error analysis). Suppose the regression function has nonhomogeneous regularities, e.g., Besov functions or functions of bounded variation. Parallel neural networks trained with weight decay achieves near optimal estimation, indicating the adaptability of parallel neural networks.

Strength And Weaknesses:
============= Strength =============

The paper is clearly written with both theoretical and empirical results. The theoretical results are correct and empirical results are supportive.

The adaptability of neural networks is studied in many recent works, including its adaptability to data geometry and adaptability to structures in function spaces. This paper considers parallel neural networks and uses weight decay for controlling the complexity of the network, which circumvents sparsity constraints in many existing network approximation results.

============= Weakness =============

There is no related work section. Some of papers share similar ideas to parallel neural networks, for example, "Approximation and Non-parametric Estimation of ResNet-type Convolutional Neural Networks" and "Besov Function Approximation and Binary Classification on Low-Dimensional Manifolds Using Convolutional Residual Networks" both rely on implementing block sparse feedforward networks. The resulting (residual) convolutional network does not need a sparsity constraint in training.

The obtained rate of estimation is slightly slower than the optimal rate, which is attainable (up to log factors) in many recent works on using ReLU neural networks for nonparametric regression.

From experiments, parallel neural networks seem to achieve comparable performance to neural networks and conventional methods.

Clarity Quality Novelty And Reproducibility:
Some of the technical contributions need highlighting. For example, the conversion of weight decay objective to the constraint form in Equation (5) can be highlighted as a contribution -- if it does not appear in Parhi & Nowak (2021a). Another example is the decomposition of \mathcal{F}_{\parallel} and \mathcal{F}_{\perp}.

A discussion on why parallel neural networks can only achieve the optimal rate in the asymptotic sense ($L \to \infty$) is helpful. Is it because the weight decay introduces additional bias?

In Theorem 1, it is good to state how to choose weight decay $\lambda$.

Lemma 6 is relative difficult to understand, mainly because the first sentence begins with an equation.

Overall, the paper is well written. There are some typos and typeset issues, for example, "errorand" --> "error and" above Section 4.2

Summary Of The Review:
I think the paper is interesting. I am currently giving a slightly negative rating, due to some concerns of the results and missing references. I am happy to raise to a positive rating after paper revision and an effective author feedback.

Rating:
6: marginally above the acceptance threshold
reviews.text:
## Peer Review Report

**Paper Summary:**

This paper presents a thorough analysis of parallel neural networks for nonparametric regression. The authors focus on examining their approximation and regression error properties, particularly for functions exhibiting nonhomogeneous regularities, such as those found in Besov or BV spaces. A key finding is that weight-decayed parallel neural networks can achieve near-optimal estimation rates, demonstrating an impressive degree of adaptability to varying function smoothness.

**Strengths:**

The manuscript is well-written and clear, presenting a compelling blend of both theoretical and empirical results. The theoretical findings appear sound, and the empirical experiments provide supportive evidence for the proposed methods. The study's focus on neural network adaptability is particularly timely and highly relevant to current research trends in machine learning theory. A notable strength is the ingenious use of weight decay to control network complexity, which effectively circumvents the often-restrictive sparsity constraints typically required in many existing neural network approximation analyses.

**Weaknesses:**

Despite its strengths, the paper has several areas that could be improved. A significant concern is the absence of a dedicated related work section. This omission is particularly noticeable regarding a discussion of papers with similar ideas, such as "Approximation and Non-parametric Estimation of ResNet-type Convolutional Neural Networks" and "Besov Function Approximation and Binary Classification on Low-Dimensional Manifolds Using Convolutional Residual Networks." These works also rely on block sparse feedforward networks and do not necessitate sparsity constraints during training, making a comparative discussion essential for contextualizing the current contributions.

Furthermore, while the obtained rate of estimation is strong, it is noted as being slightly slower than the optimal rates (up to log factors) achievable by other recent works employing ReLU neural networks for nonparametric regression. Empirically, the parallel neural networks demonstrate performance comparable to, but not clearly superior than, vanilla neural networks and conventional methods, which might temper expectations regarding their practical advantage.

**Suggestions for Improvement:**

To enhance the clarity and impact of the paper, I offer the following suggestions:

1.  **Highlight Technical Contributions:** The authors should explicitly highlight specific technical contributions. For instance, the conversion of the weight decay objective to the constraint form (Equation 5) and the decomposition of F\_parallel and F\_perp are identified as key technical elements. Their novelty or specific utility in the context of this paper should be clarified and emphasized.
2.  **Asymptotic Optimality Discussion:** Include a discussion on why parallel neural networks can only achieve the optimal rate in the asymptotic sense (as L approaches infinity). This should also address whether the use of weight decay introduces any additional bias.
3.  **Parameter Choice in Theorem 1:** Explicitly state how to choose the weight decay parameter (lambda) in Theorem 1 to ensure reproducibility and clarity for readers.
4.  **Clarity of Lemma 6:** Improve the clarity of Lemma 6, particularly its first sentence, which currently begins with an equation and is somewhat difficult to parse. Rephrasing this sentence would greatly aid understanding.
5.  **Typographical Corrections:** Address identified typos and typesetting issues throughout the manuscript. For example, "errorand" should be corrected to "error and" above Section 4.2.

**Overall Assessment and Recommendation:**

This paper presents a solid theoretical and empirical analysis of weight-decayed parallel neural networks, effectively demonstrating their local adaptivity and near-optimal estimation capabilities for functions with heterogeneous smoothness. The theoretical soundness and empirical support are commendable. However, the oversight of highly relevant prior work, the slightly suboptimal estimation rates compared to the absolute state-of-the-art, and the lack of clearly superior empirical performance are significant concerns that need to be addressed. Addressing the technical clarifications and minor presentation issues will further strengthen the manuscript.

Given the strong theoretical foundation and the relevance of the problem, I believe the paper is marginally above the acceptance threshold, provided the authors comprehensively address the identified weaknesses and suggestions.

**Rating:** 6: marginally above the acceptance threshold
reviews.original_text:
Summary Of The Paper:
The paper analyzes parallel neural networks for nonparametric regression (including approximation error and regression error analysis). Suppose the regression function has nonhomogeneous regularities, e.g., Besov functions or functions of bounded variation. Parallel neural networks trained with weight decay achieves near optimal estimation, indicating the adaptability of parallel neural networks.

Strength And Weaknesses:
============= Strength =============

The paper is clearly written with both theoretical and empirical results. The theoretical results are correct and empirical results are supportive.

The adaptability of neural networks is studied in many recent works, including its adaptability to data geometry and adaptability to structures in function spaces. This paper considers parallel neural networks and uses weight decay for controlling the complexity of the network, which circumvents sparsity constraints in many existing network approximation results.

============= Weakness =============

There is no related work section. Some of papers share similar ideas to parallel neural networks, for example, "Approximation and Non-parametric Estimation of ResNet-type Convolutional Neural Networks" and "Besov Function Approximation and Binary Classification on Low-Dimensional Manifolds Using Convolutional Residual Networks" both rely on implementing block sparse feedforward networks. The resulting (residual) convolutional network does not need a sparsity constraint in training.

The obtained rate of estimation is slightly slower than the optimal rate, which is attainable (up to log factors) in many recent works on using ReLU neural networks for nonparametric regression.

From experiments, parallel neural networks seem to achieve comparable performance to neural networks and conventional methods.

Clarity Quality Novelty And Reproducibility:
Some of the technical contributions need highlighting. For example, the conversion of weight decay objective to the constraint form in Equation (5) can be highlighted as a contribution -- if it does not appear in Parhi & Nowak (2021a). Another example is the decomposition of \mathcal{F}_{\parallel} and \mathcal{F}_{\perp}.

A discussion on why parallel neural networks can only achieve the optimal rate in the asymptotic sense ($L \to \infty$) is helpful. Is it because the weight decay introduces additional bias?

In Theorem 1, it is good to state how to choose weight decay $\lambda$.

Lemma 6 is relative difficult to understand, mainly because the first sentence begins with an equation.

Overall, the paper is well written. There are some typos and typeset issues, for example, "errorand" --> "error and" above Section 4.2

Summary Of The Review:
I think the paper is interesting. I am currently giving a slightly negative rating, due to some concerns of the results and missing references. I am happy to raise to a positive rating after paper revision and an effective author feedback.

Rating:
6: marginally above the acceptance threshold

reviews.summary_of_the_paper: This paper proposes to analyze the theory of deep neural network from the perspective of non-parametric regression. The authors show that training an $L$ layer neural network with weight decay is basically the same as penalized regression. And in the case of functions in the Besov space and bounded variation space, neural networks achieves almost near-optimal rates. When the number of layers increase, the error decreases and thus explains why deep networks performs better than shallow ones. The authors also validate some of their claims using experiments.
reviews.strength_and_weaknesses:
Strength:
  1. The paper is well-written and mostly clear.
  2. The theoretical analysis provided by the authors are, as far as I am aware of, novel and interesting.
  3. The obtained results do provide some insights on, for example, why deep networks do no overfit and why deep networks perform better than shallow networks.

Weaknesses:
  
 First off, I want to say that I am very new to the area of non-parametric regression and the function spaces the authors use in this paper. I have some experience with deep learning theory but this direction is completely new to me. Therefore, I might not be the best reviewer for this paper. Some of my comments might be unfair/wrong and I hope the authors and the other reviewers can help me understand this paper a little more during the discussion period. 

  Now back to this paper, I do have some questions about the setting and the results the authors provide. 
  
  1. The authors have chosen to optimize a set of parallel feedforward networks with $L_2$ regularization (I don't really like using the word "weight decay" here because there is no mentioning of the optimizer, e.g. SGD/Adam, and thus weight decay does not really exist). I wonder why the authors didn't include the regularization for the bias in Eqn. (2)? After looking at the Appendix, I think the authors do analyze the network with the bias term, so why is the regularization for bias missing? I think in practice, people have both regularization for weights and bias.

  2. In Theorem 1, I am confused by the statement "with proper choice of the parameter of weight decay $\lambda$". How large does the parameter need to be? Does it need to be time-varying, for example? If the choice of $\lambda$ needs to depend on the other parameters such as $M, L, p, q$, please state it explicitly in the theorems so that we can understand it more clearly.
  
  3. I am a little confused by the results in Corollary 2 and 3. For example, in Cor 2, although the $o(1)$ term is negligible, does it mean that the rate of deep neural network is worse than that of traditional nonparametric regression models? Are there models that can achieve the minimax rates that the authors have state Page 3? I think it might be better to have a table to compare the results in this paper with the results in the literature. Also, what is going to happen if $L > 100 C \log n$? For very deep neural networks, do the theorems still hold?

  4. For Cor 3, although I know that most of the paper is focused on $L\gg 2$, the authors might want to mention that the results here fail for $L=1, 2$. Also, the claim that "DNN has an advantage over kernels" is a little confusing, shouldn't there be an assumption on $L$ (L> some function of m) so that the bound for DNN is strictly better than that of kernels? I hope the authors can state it more clearly.

  I do not have enough time to go through all the proof details, but the proof overview section does give me enough intuition of how the theorems are derived. The experimental results are a good supplement to the theory.
reviews.clarity_quality_novelty_and_reproducibil: The paper is mostly clear, novel, and reproducible with the appendix.
reviews.summary_of_the_review: Overall, this seems to be a good paper with nice contributions. However, I am confused by some settings and theoretical results of the authors. Again, I am no expert in this field, and if there is anything wrong in my review, please correct me and I am happy to increase my score.
reviews.confidence: 2: You are willing to defend your assessment, but it is quite likely that you did not understand the central parts of the submission or that you are unfamiliar with some pieces of related work. Math/other details were not carefully checked.
reviews.full_review_text:
Summary Of The Paper:
This paper proposes to analyze the theory of deep neural network from the perspective of non-parametric regression. The authors show that training an $L$ layer neural network with weight decay is basically the same as penalized regression. And in the case of functions in the Besov space and bounded variation space, neural networks achieves almost near-optimal rates. When the number of layers increase, the error decreases and thus explains why deep networks performs better than shallow ones. The authors also validate some of their claims using experiments.

Strength And Weaknesses:
Strength:
  1. The paper is well-written and mostly clear.
  2. The theoretical analysis provided by the authors are, as far as I am aware of, novel and interesting.
  3. The obtained results do provide some insights on, for example, why deep networks do no overfit and why deep networks perform better than shallow networks.

Weaknesses:
  
 First off, I want to say that I am very new to the area of non-parametric regression and the function spaces the authors use in this paper. I have some experience with deep learning theory but this direction is completely new to me. Therefore, I might not be the best reviewer for this paper. Some of my comments might be unfair/wrong and I hope the authors and the other reviewers can help me understand this paper a little more during the discussion period. 

  Now back to this paper, I do have some questions about the setting and the results the authors provide. 
  
  1. The authors have chosen to optimize a set of parallel feedforward networks with $L_2$ regularization (I don't really like using the word "weight decay" here because there is no mentioning of the optimizer, e.g. SGD/Adam, and thus weight decay does not really exist). I wonder why the authors didn't include the regularization for the bias in Eqn. (2)? After looking at the Appendix, I think the authors do analyze the network with the bias term, so why is the regularization for bias missing? I think in practice, people have both regularization for weights and bias.

  2. In Theorem 1, I am confused by the statement "with proper choice of the parameter of weight decay $\lambda$". How large does the parameter need to be? Does it need to be time-varying, for example? If the choice of $\lambda$ needs to depend on the other parameters such as $M, L, p, q$, please state it explicitly in the theorems so that we can understand it more clearly.
  
  3. I am a little confused by the results in Corollary 2 and 3. For example, in Cor 2, although the $o(1)$ term is negligible, does it mean that the rate of deep neural network is worse than that of traditional nonparametric regression models? Are there models that can achieve the minimax rates that the authors have state Page 3? I think it might be better to have a table to compare the results in this paper with the results in the literature. Also, what is going to happen if $L > 100 C \log n$? For very deep neural networks, do the theorems still hold?

  4. For Cor 3, although I know that most of the paper is focused on $L\gg 2$, the authors might want to mention that the results here fail for $L=1, 2$. Also, the claim that "DNN has an advantage over kernels" is a little confusing, shouldn't there be an assumption on $L$ (L> some function of m) so that the bound for DNN is strictly better than that of kernels? I hope the authors can state it more clearly.

  I do not have enough time to go through all the proof details, but the proof overview section does give me enough intuition of how the theorems are derived. The experimental results are a good supplement to the theory.

Clarity Quality Novelty And Reproducibility:
The paper is mostly clear, novel, and reproducible with the appendix.

reviews.text:
## Peer Review Report

**Paper Title:** [Assumed Title - not provided in JSON, but implied by content]

**Overall Assessment:** 6: marginally above the acceptance threshold

This paper presents a novel and interesting theoretical analysis of deep neural networks (DNNs) from a non-parametric regression perspective. The core contribution lies in demonstrating that training an L-layer neural network with L2 regularization is equivalent to penalized regression, thereby offering significant insights into why deep networks may not overfit and why they often outperform shallow architectures. The theoretical claims, which suggest that these networks can achieve almost near-optimal rates for functions in Besov and bounded variation spaces, are complemented by experimental validation. While the paper's contributions are valuable, several areas require clarification and refinement to enhance its precision and impact.

### Strengths

*   **Novel and Insightful Theoretical Analysis:** The theoretical framework connecting L-layer neural networks with L2 regularization to penalized regression is novel and provides a deeper understanding of DNNs' non-parametric properties.
*   **Explanation for Deep Network Performance:** The obtained results offer compelling theoretical insights into two key aspects of deep learning: why deep networks tend to generalize well (do not overfit) and why increasing depth often leads to improved performance compared to shallow networks.
*   **Clarity and Readability:** The paper is generally well-written and clear, making the complex theoretical arguments accessible to a broad audience.
*   **Experimental Support:** The inclusion of experimental validation is a valuable complement to the theoretical contributions, strengthening the paper's overall argument.

### Weaknesses

While the theoretical contributions are strong, several points require attention to improve the paper's rigor and clarity:

*   **Imprecise Terminology for Regularization:** The term "weight decay" is used without specific mention of the optimizer (e.g., SGD/Adam), which can lead to imprecision as its practical implementation can vary.
*   **Inconsistent Bias Regularization:** Equation (2) omits regularization for the bias term, despite its analysis in the Appendix and its common practice in neural network training. This inconsistency should be addressed for completeness.
*   **Ambiguity of Regularization Parameter (Lambda):** There is confusion regarding the "proper choice" of the regularization parameter lambda in Theorem 1. Specifically, it's unclear whether its magnitude, time-variance, or dependence on other parameters (M, L, p, q) needs to be explicitly stated for clarity.
*   **Unclear Implications of Corollaries 2 and 3:** The implications of Corollaries 2 and 3 regarding the comparative performance of deep neural networks are not entirely clear. It is ambiguous whether the stated deep neural network rate is worse than traditional nonparametric regression models or if other models achieve the stated minimax rates. The 'o(1)' term in Corollary 2 also needs careful consideration regarding its effect on minimax rates.
*   **Applicability to Very Deep Networks:** The applicability of the theorems to very deep neural networks (e.g., L > 100 C log n) is not clearly articulated, leaving questions about the practical bounds of the theoretical findings.
*   **Conditions for DNN Advantage over Kernels:** The claim in Corollary 3 that DNNs have an advantage over kernels lacks explicit assumptions on L (e.g., L > some function of m) to clearly demonstrate a strictly better bound. The results in Corollary 3 may also not hold for small L values (L=1, 2).

### Suggestions for Improvement

To address the identified weaknesses and further enhance the paper's quality, the following suggestions are offered:

1.  **Clarify Lambda's Dependence:** Explicitly state if the choice of lambda depends on other parameters (M, L, p, q) within the theorems for enhanced clarity and reproducibility.
2.  **Comparative Results Table:** Provide a table to compare the results presented in the paper with existing literature, particularly concerning Corollaries 2 and 3. This would help clarify the comparative performance of DNNs against traditional nonparametric regression models and minimax rates.
3.  **Specify L Conditions for Corollary 3:** Mention that the results in Corollary 3 may not hold for small L values (e.g., L=1, 2) and more clearly state the assumptions on L required for DNNs to demonstrate a strict advantage over kernel methods. This would provide a more nuanced understanding of the conditions under which the claimed advantage holds.
4.  **Refine Regularization Terminology:** Ensure consistent and precise terminology for regularization, especially when discussing "weight decay" in relation to specific optimizers or implementations.
5.  **Address Bias Regularization:** Either include bias regularization in Equation (2) or provide a clear justification for its omission in the main equation while it is analyzed in the Appendix.

### Conclusion

This paper presents a significant theoretical contribution by framing deep neural networks as penalized regression and deriving near-optimal estimation rates. The insights into the generalization and performance benefits of deep architectures are particularly valuable. While the identified weaknesses regarding clarity in theoretical setup, hyperparameter tuning, and the implications of comparative rates need to be addressed, they do not fundamentally undermine the paper's core contributions. With revisions to enhance precision and provide clearer comparative context, this work has the potential to be a strong addition to the literature. Therefore, I recommend its acceptance, provided the authors address the raised concerns.
reviews.original_text:
Summary Of The Paper:
This paper proposes to analyze the theory of deep neural network from the perspective of non-parametric regression. The authors show that training an $L$ layer neural network with weight decay is basically the same as penalized regression. And in the case of functions in the Besov space and bounded variation space, neural networks achieves almost near-optimal rates. When the number of layers increase, the error decreases and thus explains why deep networks performs better than shallow ones. The authors also validate some of their claims using experiments.

Strength And Weaknesses:
Strength:
  1. The paper is well-written and mostly clear.
  2. The theoretical analysis provided by the authors are, as far as I am aware of, novel and interesting.
  3. The obtained results do provide some insights on, for example, why deep networks do no overfit and why deep networks perform better than shallow networks.

Weaknesses:
  
 First off, I want to say that I am very new to the area of non-parametric regression and the function spaces the authors use in this paper. I have some experience with deep learning theory but this direction is completely new to me. Therefore, I might not be the best reviewer for this paper. Some of my comments might be unfair/wrong and I hope the authors and the other reviewers can help me understand this paper a little more during the discussion period. 

  Now back to this paper, I do have some questions about the setting and the results the authors provide. 
  
  1. The authors have chosen to optimize a set of parallel feedforward networks with $L_2$ regularization (I don't really like using the word "weight decay" here because there is no mentioning of the optimizer, e.g. SGD/Adam, and thus weight decay does not really exist). I wonder why the authors didn't include the regularization for the bias in Eqn. (2)? After looking at the Appendix, I think the authors do analyze the network with the bias term, so why is the regularization for bias missing? I think in practice, people have both regularization for weights and bias.

  2. In Theorem 1, I am confused by the statement "with proper choice of the parameter of weight decay $\lambda$". How large does the parameter need to be? Does it need to be time-varying, for example? If the choice of $\lambda$ needs to depend on the other parameters such as $M, L, p, q$, please state it explicitly in the theorems so that we can understand it more clearly.
  
  3. I am a little confused by the results in Corollary 2 and 3. For example, in Cor 2, although the $o(1)$ term is negligible, does it mean that the rate of deep neural network is worse than that of traditional nonparametric regression models? Are there models that can achieve the minimax rates that the authors have state Page 3? I think it might be better to have a table to compare the results in this paper with the results in the literature. Also, what is going to happen if $L > 100 C \log n$? For very deep neural networks, do the theorems still hold?

  4. For Cor 3, although I know that most of the paper is focused on $L\gg 2$, the authors might want to mention that the results here fail for $L=1, 2$. Also, the claim that "DNN has an advantage over kernels" is a little confusing, shouldn't there be an assumption on $L$ (L> some function of m) so that the bound for DNN is strictly better than that of kernels? I hope the authors can state it more clearly.

  I do not have enough time to go through all the proof details, but the proof overview section does give me enough intuition of how the theorems are derived. The experimental results are a good supplement to the theory.

Clarity Quality Novelty And Reproducibility:
The paper is mostly clear, novel, and reproducible with the appendix.

Summary Of The Review:
Overall, this seems to be a good paper with nice contributions. However, I am confused by some settings and theoretical results of the authors. Again, I am no expert in this field, and if there is anything wrong in my review, please correct me and I am happy to increase my score.

Rating:
6: marginally above the acceptance threshold

reviews.summary_of_the_paper: This paper extends Suzuki’s work (ICLR2018) to parallel neural networks in the Besov space as well as BV functions, studies the generalization properties in terms of approximation error via B-spline basis and estimation error via covering number, and demonstrates the $\ell_2$ norm (weight decay) in parallel ReLU neural networks is equivalent to a certain sparse regularization term.
reviews.strength_and_weaknesses:
**Pros:** 

1. This work allows for over-parameterization without the extra sparsity condition
The obtained bound of the covering number is independent of the number of subnetworks, which is substituted by the Frobenius norm.  

**Cons:**
 
1. The motivation on locally adaptivity makes sense, comparing linear estimators and DNNs. Nevertheless, the motivation on the comparison of (Suzuki ICLR2019)  is relatively weak. The authors claim that Suzuki’s work requires certain conditions on the width, the depth, the sparsity to achieve the minimax rate on deep ReLU networks, which is different from practical uses. Nevertheless, this work does not focus on this issue but centers around another architecture, i.e., parallel neural networks. Even though parallel neural networks have theoretical and empirical applications as the authors suggested, the “correct” motivation is to solve certain conditions in Suzuki’s work under the same architecture.

More importantly, this work also requires several conditions on the width, the depth, etc. In the “No architecture tuning” part, the required architecture of the model still depends on $\alpha$, see Theorem 1. It is hard for me to accept the authors’ motivation.


2. In theorem 1, the number of subnetworks is exponentially large enough w.r.t the input dimension $d$. This is unacceptable in both theory and practice.


3. In Proposition 4, the equivalence between Eq. (2) and Eq. (12) is imprecise via the Lagrangian’s method in optimization. The constraint value P doesn't appear in Eq. (2) and it is also not clear how the dual variable is handled. In fact P (as well as P’) is unknown or uncomputable, which also makes the upper bound of the covering number appear infeasible in Theorem 5 depending on P’. It is ok for ease of description but is not rigorous in a theory paper corresponding to the first contribution.

4. After reading this paper, I think the technical contribution is to derive the covering number bound of the parallel neural networks for the estimation error, i.e., Theorem 5 and Lemma 6. The proof for approximation error follows Yarotsky (2017); Suzuki (2018). However, this paper works under a fixed design, which is much easier to derive the estimation error.
reviews.clarity_quality_novelty_and_reproducibil:
This paper is well-written. Apart from the above mentioned issues, I have other questions that

1. I agree with the authors that kernel methods, e.g., NTK, lacks local adaptivity to some extent due to RKHS. I’m wondering that, the mean field regime (instead of NTK) is also suboptimal to local adaptivity?


2. For the random design, the proof of estimation error can be similarly established by [1]?

[1] Schmidt-Hieber, Johannes. "Nonparametric regression using deep neural networks with ReLU activation function." The Annals of Statistics 48, no. 4 (2020): 1875-1897.

Nevertheless, the derived results appear vacuous in terms of imprecise Lagrangian dual and curse of dimensionality of the input dimension.

Summary Of The Paper:
This paper extends Suzuki’s work (ICLR2018) to parallel neural networks in the Besov space as well as BV functions, studies the generalization properties in terms of approximation error via B-spline basis and estimation error via covering number, and demonstrates the $\ell_2$ norm (weight decay) in parallel ReLU neural networks is equivalent to a certain sparse regularization term.

Strength And Weaknesses:
**Pros:** 

1. This work allows for over-parameterization without the extra sparsity condition
The obtained bound of the covering number is independent of the number of subnetworks, which is substituted by the Frobenius norm.  

**Cons:**
 
1. The motivation on locally adaptivity makes sense, comparing linear estimators and DNNs. Nevertheless, the motivation on the comparison of (Suzuki ICLR2019)  is relatively weak. The authors claim that Suzuki’s work requires certain conditions on the width, the depth, the sparsity to achieve the minimax rate on deep ReLU networks, which is different from practical uses. Nevertheless, this work does not focus on this issue but centers around another architecture, i.e., parallel neural networks. Even though parallel neural networks have theoretical and empirical applications as the authors suggested, the “correct” motivation is to solve certain conditions in Suzuki’s work under the same architecture.

More importantly, this work also requires several conditions on the width, the depth, etc. In the “No architecture tuning” part, the required architecture of the model still depends on $\alpha$, see Theorem 1. It is hard for me to accept the authors’ motivation.


2. In theorem 1, the number of subnetworks is exponentially large enough w.r.t the input dimension $d$. This is unacceptable in both theory and practice.


3. In Proposition 4, the equivalence between Eq. (2) and Eq. (12) is imprecise via the Lagrangian’s method in optimization. The constraint value P doesn't appear in Eq. (2) and it is also not clear how the dual variable is handled. In fact P (as well as P’) is unknown or uncomputable, which also makes the upper bound of the covering number appear infeasible in Theorem 5 depending on P’. It is ok for ease of description but is not rigorous in a theory paper corresponding to the first contribution.

4. After reading this paper, I think the technical contribution is to derive the covering number bound of the parallel neural networks for the estimation error, i.e., Theorem 5 and Lemma 6. The proof for approximation error follows Yarotsky (2017); Suzuki (2018). However, this paper works under a fixed design, which is much easier to derive the estimation error.

Clarity Quality Novelty And Reproducibility:
This paper is well-written. Apart from the above mentioned issues, I have other questions that

1. I agree with the authors that kernel methods, e.g., NTK, lacks local adaptivity to some extent due to RKHS. I’m wondering that, the mean field regime (instead of NTK) is also suboptimal to local adaptivity?


2. For the random design, the proof of estimation error can be similarly established by [1]?

[1] Schmidt-Hieber, Johannes. "Nonparametric regression using deep neural networks with ReLU activation function." The Annals of Statistics 48, no. 4 (2020): 1875-1897.

Summary Of The Review:
This work has some good results on the sparsity, the separation between linear estimators and DNNs in Besov spaces as well as BV functions. Nevertheless, the derived results appear vacuous in terms of imprecise Lagrangian dual and curse of dimensionality of the input dimension.

Rating:
5: marginally below the acceptance threshold
reviews.text:
## Peer Review

**Overall Rating:** 5: marginally below the acceptance threshold

This paper extends previous work by Suzuki (ICLR2018) to the setting of parallel neural networks, specifically analyzing their generalization properties for functions in Besov spaces and BV functions. The authors investigate both approximation error, utilizing a B-spline basis, and estimation error, derived via covering number arguments. A key theoretical contribution is the demonstration of an equivalence between ℓ2 regularization (weight decay) in parallel ReLU neural networks and a certain sparse regularization term. Furthermore, the work highlights a separation between linear estimators and DNNs in Besov and BV spaces, contributing to our understanding of deep learning's theoretical advantages.

**Strengths:**

The paper offers several commendable theoretical insights. Firstly, it provides a framework that allows for over-parameterization without necessitating an additional sparsity condition, which is a significant theoretical advantage. Secondly, the obtained bound for the covering number is notably independent of the number of subnetworks, instead being expressed in terms of the Frobenius norm, simplifying analysis. The results presented on sparsity are good, and the formal demonstration of a separation between linear estimators and DNNs in Besov and BV function spaces is a valuable theoretical contribution.

**Weaknesses:**

Despite these strengths, the paper suffers from several substantial weaknesses that collectively place it marginally below the acceptance threshold.

1.  **Weak Motivation:** The motivation for this work, particularly in comparison to Suzuki (ICLR2019), is weak. The paper shifts to parallel neural networks rather than directly addressing and solving specific conditions within Suzuki’s original single-network architecture, which could have provided a clearer and more impactful research trajectory.

2.  **Impractical Architectural Assumptions:**
    *   Theorem 1 states that the required architecture (specifically, width and depth) still depends on the parameter α. This dependency contradicts the paper's claim of "No architecture tuning" and significantly weakens the adaptivity motivation.
    *   Furthermore, Theorem 1 mandates an exponentially large number of subnetworks with respect to the input dimension 'd'. This requirement is highly impractical, both theoretically and for any potential real-world implementation.

3.  **Technical Imprecisions and Uncomputable Parameters:**
    *   The equivalence claimed between Eq. (2) and Eq. (12) in Proposition 4 is imprecise. There are issues with the application of Lagrangian's method, and the handling of dual variables is not sufficiently clear, leading to a lack of rigor.
    *   In Theorem 5, which deals with the upper bound of the covering number, the constraint value P (and P') is stated as unknown or uncomputable. This renders the derived upper bound infeasible in practice, undermining its utility.

4.  **Limited Technical Challenge:** The derivation of the estimation error relies on a fixed design. While valid, this approach is generally considered less technically challenging compared to establishing similar results for a random design problem.

**Suggestions for Improvement:**

1.  **Refocus Motivation:** The authors should consider refocusing the paper's motivation to directly address and resolve certain specific conditions or limitations identified in Suzuki’s earlier work, but within the *same* neural network architecture context. This would provide a more direct and compelling argument for the current paper's necessity.

2.  **Address Random Design:** Given the current reliance on a fixed design for estimation error, it would significantly strengthen the paper if the authors could clarify whether the proof of estimation error for the more challenging random design problem can be similarly established. Referencing or drawing parallels with works like Schmidt-Hieber (2020) could be beneficial here.

**Conclusion:**

The paper presents interesting theoretical contributions regarding sparsity and the distinction between DNNs and linear estimators in Besov/BV spaces. The independence of the covering number bound from the number of subnetworks is also a notable aspect. However, the work is significantly hampered by a weak motivation, impractical architectural assumptions (especially the exponential number of subnetworks), and critical imprecisions in the theoretical derivations concerning Lagrangian duality and the reliance on uncomputable parameters for key bounds. These weaknesses are substantial enough to warrant a decision of "marginally below the acceptance threshold" in its current form. Addressing the highlighted points would greatly enhance the paper's rigor and impact.

title: Tensor-Based Sketching Method for the Low-Rank Approximation of Data Streams.

reviews.summary_of_the_paper: In this work, the authors summarized previous algorithms to recover column/row spaces of a set of matrices fast and introduced Tucker decomposition of tensor into them, which can give a good choice of hyperparameter in those algorithms.
reviews.strength_and_weaknesses:
Strength: in those previous algorithms, they require the approximation for matrices in the training set to be as accurate as possible and this work provides the method based on Tucker decomposition with theoretical analysis. And the idea of viewing a stream of matrices as a tensor is novel and helpful in finding their common subspaces. 

Weakness: the analysis is not complete. It didn’t mention how close is S, the estimator of U_k, in general cases to U_k. Also the bound in theorem 2 is not clear with m and n.
reviews.clarity_quality_novelty_and_reproducibil: The quality is fine. Some definitions are not so clear, e.g. in (1.1) where the authors define B, is this B for all d, or it should be B_d for different d? It is pretty novel.
reviews.summary_of_the_review: This work should qualify for a conference paper, although there are something which can be improved.
reviews.confidence: 3: You are fairly confident in your assessment. It is possible that you did not understand some parts of the submission or that you are unfamiliar with some pieces of related work. Math/other details were not carefully checked.
reviews.full_review_text:
Summary Of The Paper:
In this work, the authors summarized previous algorithms to recover column/row spaces of a set of matrices fast and introduced Tucker decomposition of tensor into them, which can give a good choice of hyperparameter in those algorithms.

Strength And Weaknesses:
Strength: in those previous algorithms, they require the approximation for matrices in the training set to be as accurate as possible and this work provides the method based on Tucker decomposition with theoretical analysis. And the idea of viewing a stream of matrices as a tensor is novel and helpful in finding their common subspaces. 

Weakness: the analysis is not complete. It didn’t mention how close is S, the estimator of U_k, in general cases to U_k. Also the bound in theorem 2 is not clear with m and n.

Clarity Quality Novelty And Reproducibility:
The quality is fine. Some definitions are not so clear, e.g. in (1.1) where the authors define B, is this B for all d, or it should be B_d for different d? It is pretty novel.

Summary Of The Review:
This work should qualify for a conference paper, although there are something which can be improved.

6: marginally above the acceptance threshold
reviews.text:
## Peer Review

**Overall Recommendation: 6: marginally above the acceptance threshold**

This paper introduces a novel tensor-based sketching method utilizing Tucker decomposition for low-rank approximation in data streams. A key strength of the proposed approach is its theoretically analyzed method for selecting hyperparameters, which is a valuable contribution to the field. The core idea of viewing a stream of matrices as a tensor is particularly innovative and helpful for identifying common subspaces within the data stream. Overall, the work demonstrates a commendable level of novelty and the paper's quality is generally fine.

However, despite these strengths, the paper currently suffers from several significant theoretical and clarity issues that warrant revision.

**Strengths:**

*   **Novelty and Approach:** The paper presents a novel approach by leveraging Tucker decomposition within a tensor-based sketching framework for low-rank approximation in data streams. The concept of treating a stream of matrices as a tensor to uncover common subspaces is particularly insightful and represents a fresh perspective.
*   **Theoretical Analysis for Hyperparameter Selection:** The method provides a theoretically analyzed approach for accurate matrix approximation in training sets, which is crucial for practical application and distinguishes it from purely heuristic methods.
*   **General Quality:** The overall quality of the paper is fine, and the work introduces a pretty novel idea to the research community.

**Weaknesses and Suggestions for Improvement:**

The primary criticism revolves around the incompleteness and lack of clarity in certain aspects of the theoretical analysis and definitions. Addressing these points would significantly strengthen the paper's rigor and impact.

1.  **Incomplete Theoretical Analysis:**
    *   **Weakness:** The analysis currently does not clearly articulate how close `S` (the estimator of `U_k`) is to `U_k` in general cases. A more complete theoretical understanding of this closeness is essential for assessing the estimator's reliability.
    *   **Suggestion:** The authors should complete this analysis, providing a clear explanation and bounds regarding the closeness of `S` to `U_k` under general conditions.
2.  **Ambiguity in Theorem 2 Bound:**
    *   **Weakness:** The bound presented in Theorem 2 is not sufficiently clear, specifically concerning its dependence on `m` and `n`. This lack of clarity hinders a full understanding of the bound's implications and how it scales with problem dimensions.
    *   **Suggestion:** Please clarify the bound in Theorem 2, explicitly stating and explaining its dependence on `m` and `n`.
3.  **Unclear Definitions:**
    *   **Weakness:** There is ambiguity in some definitions, particularly regarding the variable `B` in equation (1.1). It is not clear whether `B` applies uniformly across all dimensions `d` or if it represents `B_d` for different `d`.
    *   **Suggestion:** The authors should clarify the definition of `B` in equation (1.1), specifying its scope and intended meaning (e.g., whether it is a single value or dimension-specific).

**Main Points Summary:**

The paper proposes a novel tensor-based sketching method using Tucker decomposition for low-rank approximation in data streams, offering a theoretically supported approach for improving hyperparameter selection. While the core idea is novel and valuable, a primary criticism is the incompleteness of the theoretical analysis, particularly concerning the general closeness of the estimator `S` to `U_k` and the clarity of bounds in Theorem 2. Additionally, issues with the clarity of certain definitions, such as the variable 'B' in equation (1.1), need to be resolved.

**Conclusion:**

The paper presents a promising and novel method with a strong underlying idea. The theoretical analysis for hyperparameter selection is a welcome addition. However, the identified issues concerning the completeness of the theoretical analysis and the clarity of definitions are non-trivial and need to be thoroughly addressed. With these revisions, the paper's theoretical foundation and overall clarity would be significantly enhanced, making it a stronger contribution to the literature. I recommend acceptance, provided these crucial revisions are made.

reviews.summary_of_the_paper:
The paper studies the data-driven low-rank approximation problem on data streams, which has gained attention recently. For a single matrix, some fast algorithms for low-rank approximation are based on sketching, and specifically they choose a sparse sketching matrix with random signs, which is multiplied by the original matrix to reduce its row and/or column dimensions while still allowing approximate recovery of the best low-rank approximation. In a stream of matrices assumed to be related to each other, it has been recently shown that the entries of the sketching matrix can be chosen in a data-driven way based on matrices previously seen on the stream instead of using random signs, in order to improve performance.

This paper suggests a new algorithm along these lines, that views the stream of matrices as a tensor of order 3, which leads to a formulation of a loss problem that can be solved directly and yields an appropriate sketching matrix, albeit non-sparse.
reviews.strength_and_weaknesses:
The tensor-based approach seems interesting and may lead to useful insights on the problem. However, there is a major issue that makes the proposed algorithm incomparable to prior work, both theoretically and empirically - the lack of sparsity of the output sketching matrix. This unfortunately renders the paper methodologically unviable. 

In all prior work (both oblivious and data-driven), the sketching matrix has a certain structure that allows fast matrix multiplication by it (usually sparsity), while here no such structure is guaranteed. On the theoretical level, this makes the resulting low-rank approximation algorithm asymptotically slower than all of the prior work it is compared to.

The manuscript is aware of this issue, and finally addresses it on the empirical level, on page 8, stating that even though the asymptotic running time is slower, the proposed algorithm is in fact empirically faster than prior work based on experiments. It goes on to explain this is because the implementation of the proposed algorithm uses the built-in (highly optimized) PyTorch matrix multiplication functionality, while the baselines were implemented with the non-optimized (and inevitably much slower) sparse matrix multiplication code released by the authors of those works. This unfortunately renders the reported testing time results essentially meaningless: the algorithms should be compared in the same experimental setting (software and hardware). Using the same dense matrix multiplication functionality in PyTorch for the sparse baselines would have already improved their running time to the same as the proposed method, erasing any reported advantage in testing time.

To be clear, it is true that as of now, PyTorch and comparable optimized software packages for matrices do not exploit sparsity, and dense matrix multiplication is as fast as sparse on the datasets considered here. Sparsity is useful theoretically (for getting better asymptotic bounds) and also in practice if the matrices are too large to fit in working memory (which makes dense matrix multiplication slow or infeasible). I do not question the reported results themselves, and one could perhaps make the argument that the sparsity of the sketching matrix is not helpful at all and could be dispensed with, but the current framing and methodological design of the paper are unviable. If sparsity is indeed unhelpful, then the sparse baseline should be implemented as dense ones and not crippled by attempting to leverage sparsity, and dense methods for low-rank approximation could be considered and reported as well (even full SVD).

reviews.full_review_text:
Summary Of The Paper:
The paper studies the data-driven low-rank approximation problem on data streams, which has gained attention recently. For a single matrix, some fast algorithms for low-rank approximation are based on sketching, and specifically they choose a sparse sketching matrix with random signs, which is multiplied by the original matrix to reduce its row and/or column dimensions while still allowing approximate recovery of the best low-rank approximation. In a stream of matrices assumed to be related to each other, it has been recently shown that the entries of the sketching matrix can be chosen in a data-driven way based on matrices previously seen on the stream instead of using random signs, in order to improve performance.

This paper suggests a new algorithm along these lines, that views the stream of matrices as a tensor of order 3, which leads to a formulation of a loss problem that can be solved directly and yields an appropriate sketching matrix, albeit non-sparse.

Strength And Weaknesses:
The tensor-based approach seems interesting and may lead to useful insights on the problem. However, there is a major issue that makes the proposed algorithm incomparable to prior work, both theoretically and empirically - the lack of sparsity of the output sketching matrix. This unfortunately renders the paper methodologically unviable. 

In all prior work (both oblivious and data-driven), the sketching matrix has a certain structure that allows fast matrix multiplication by it (usually sparsity), while here no such structure is guaranteed. On the theoretical level, this makes the resulting low-rank approximation algorithm asymptotically slower than all of the prior work it is compared to.

The manuscript is aware of this issue, and finally addresses it on the empirical level, on page 8, stating that even though the asymptotic running time is slower, the proposed algorithm is in fact empirically faster than prior work based on experiments. It goes on to explain this is because the implementation of the proposed algorithm uses the built-in (highly optimized) PyTorch matrix multiplication functionality, while the baselines were implemented with the non-optimized (and inevitably much slower) sparse matrix multiplication code released by the authors of those works. This unfortunately renders the reported testing time results essentially meaningless: the algorithms should be compared in the same experimental setting (software and hardware). Using the same dense matrix multiplication functionality in PyTorch for the sparse baselines would have already improved their running time to the same as the proposed method, erasing any reported advantage in testing time.

To be clear, it is true that as of now, PyTorch and comparable optimized software packages for matrices do not exploit sparsity, and dense matrix multiplication is as fast as sparse on the datasets considered here. Sparsity is useful theoretically (for getting better asymptotic bounds) and also in practice if the matrices are too large to fit in working memory (which makes dense matrix multiplication slow or infeasible). I do not question the reported results themselves, and one could perhaps make the argument that the sparsity of the sketching matrix is not helpful at all and could be dispensed with, but the current framing and methodological design of the paper are unviable. If sparsity is indeed unhelpful, then the sparse baseline should be implemented as dense ones and not crippled by attempting to leverage sparsity, and dense methods for low-rank approximation could be considered and reported as well (even full SVD).

Clarity Quality Novelty And Reproducibility:
Clarity: I found the proofs abbreviated and difficult to follow, and it would help to expand them and add explanations and bridging steps.
Reproducibility: I appreciate the fully disclosed details of the implementation.

Summary Of The Review:
This is a potentially interesting paper but with flaws in its framing and methodological design, with which I cannot recommend acceptance. I could have recommended accepting a version of the paper with largely the same technical material, but that clarifies properly the departure from prior work, and with a viable experimental design that compares the baselines on a level ground and includes more relevant baselines. I also recommend rewriting the proofs in a clearer way that would assist in verifying the stated theorems.

Rating:
6: marginally above the acceptance threshold
reviews.text:
This paper introduces a novel non-iterative tensor-based sketching method for low-rank approximation in data streams. The core idea of leveraging tensor methods for this problem is interesting and could potentially lead to valuable insights. The authors also deserve credit for providing fully disclosed implementation details, which significantly aids reproducibility.

However, the paper suffers from several fundamental methodological and empirical flaws that prevent its acceptance in its current form. My assessment is that while the underlying tensor-based approach holds promise, the current execution and presentation require major revisions.

**Weaknesses:**

1.  **Methodological Unviability due to Lack of Sparsity:** A primary concern is that the proposed tensor-based method yields a *non-sparse* sketching matrix. This is a critical departure from prior work (both oblivious and data-driven methods) which typically employs sparse sketching matrices to achieve faster matrix multiplication and better asymptotic bounds. Consequently, the proposed low-rank approximation algorithm is asymptotically *slower* than existing methods, making its current methodological design fundamentally unviable when compared to established sparse techniques.
2.  **Unfair Empirical Comparison:** The reported testing time results are misleading and essentially meaningless due to an unfair empirical comparison. The proposed algorithm's implementation utilizes highly optimized PyTorch dense matrix multiplication (specifically `torch.matmul()`). In contrast, the baselines were implemented using non-optimized sparse matrix multiplication code released by their original authors. This creates an unequal playing field. It's important to note that PyTorch and similar optimized software packages often do not fully exploit sparsity in a way that makes sparse matrix multiplication significantly faster than dense for the problem sizes typically considered, meaning that dense operations can appear equally fast or even faster. This invalidates the paper's empirical claims regarding performance.
3.  **Proof Clarity:** The proofs provided are abbreviated and difficult to follow. This lack of detail hinders the ability of a reviewer or reader to verify the correctness of the stated theorems.
4.  **Overall Framing:** The combination of the non-sparse nature of the method and the flawed empirical comparison renders the current framing and methodological design of the paper unviable for publication without significant changes.

**Suggestions for Improvement:**

1.  **Revamp Experimental Design:** A complete overhaul of the experimental design is imperative. All algorithms, including the proposed method and baselines, must be compared in the *same experimental setting*, ensuring consistent software, hardware, and optimization levels.
2.  **Fair Baseline Comparison:** To ensure a level playing field, if the authors wish to highlight the performance of their dense method, sparse baselines should be reimplemented as dense ones (if appropriate) for comparison. Alternatively, the paper should consider and report comparisons against more relevant dense methods for low-rank approximation, such as full SVD or other dense streaming approaches, to contextualize its performance. The paper needs to acknowledge the theoretical implications of sparsity (or lack thereof) honestly.
3.  **Clarify Departure from Prior Work:** The paper needs to properly and thoroughly clarify its departure from prior work, particularly regarding the implications of using a non-sparse sketching matrix compared to the sparse matrices typically employed in the field. This includes providing a clear justification for why a non-sparse approach is beneficial or warranted, especially given the asymptotic performance implications.
4.  **Enhance Proofs:** The proofs should be rewritten to be clearer, with expanded explanations and bridging steps, to assist in verifying the stated theorems.

**Overall Assessment:**

While the tensor-based approach presented in this paper offers an interesting direction for low-rank approximation in data streams, its current methodological design suffers from fundamental flaws related to the lack of sparsity in the sketching matrix and an invalid empirical comparison. The reported testing times are not credible due to unequal optimization levels between the proposed method and baselines. The paper, in its current state, cannot be recommended for acceptance. However, given the novelty of the core idea, I believe that with substantial revisions addressing these fundamental methodological and clarity issues, the paper could potentially become a valuable contribution. Therefore, I recommend a **conditional acceptance** pending major revisions that rigorously address all points raised.

reviews.summary_of_the_paper:
The paper introduces a low-rank tensor approximation algorithm intended for data streams applications. 
According to the authors, the algorithm fully exploits the structure of data streams and obtains quasi-optimal sketching matrices by performing tensor decomposition on training data. 
The approach computes the sketching matrix by minimizing a new loss function which is a relaxation of that in the IVY tensor decomposition method. Specifically, the key is that the minimization of this loss function by tensor decomposition on the training set is non-iterative. The authors formulate a couple of theorems that bound the approximation performance and provide empirical analysis on several real data stream datasets.
reviews.strength_and_weaknesses:
+ 

* The paper is well written and clear for the most part. It does need to be proof read for small typos and grammar.
*  The idea is well grounded in existing methods, e.g. IVY and SCW and the approximation quality is derived in two theorems.
*  The authors test their algorithm on real datasets and show some improvements. 

- 
* Overall I have some concerns regarding the novelty as the proposed algorithms are somewhat straightforward extensions of the existing algorithms, e.g. SCW.

reviews.text:
The paper introduces a novel non-iterative, tensor-based sketching method designed for low-rank approximation in data streams. The authors contend that their approach offers superior accuracy and speed compared to existing data-driven techniques. While the paper exhibits commendable clarity and technical quality, concerns regarding the novelty of the proposed algorithms slightly temper its overall contribution. Despite these reservations, the paper's strengths are sufficient to place it marginally above the acceptance threshold.

**Strengths:**

The paper is exceptionally well-written and clear, making the intricate concepts accessible to readers. The proposed methodology is solidly grounded in established techniques, drawing inspiration from methods such as IVY and SCW, which provides a strong theoretical and practical foundation. A significant strength lies in the robust theoretical analysis, where the approximation quality of the method is rigorously derived and presented in two distinct theorems. The algorithm effectively exploits the inherent structure of data streams, employing tensor decomposition on training data to obtain quasi-optimal sketching matrices. Furthermore, it introduces a new, non-iterative loss function for computing these sketching matrices, which is described as a relaxation of the IVY tensor decomposition method. Empirical evaluations on real-world datasets demonstrate clear improvements over baseline methods, underscoring the practical utility and performance benefits of the proposed approach. Overall, the paper scores highly in terms of both clarity and technical quality.

**Weaknesses:**

Despite its strengths, the paper has a few areas that warrant attention. The primary concern revolves around the perceived limited novelty of the proposed algorithms. They are largely presented as somewhat straightforward extensions of existing methods, particularly SCW, which may dilute the originality of the contribution. Additionally, while the paper is generally well-written, it would benefit from a thorough proofreading pass to correct minor typos and grammatical errors that occasionally appear. A crucial point for academic research is reproducibility; the authors have not provided code for their implementation, nor have they explicitly stated an intention to share it, which hinders independent verification and further research.

**Suggestions for Improvement:**

1.  **Proofreading:** I strongly recommend a comprehensive proofreading of the entire manuscript to address all minor typos and grammatical inconsistencies.
2.  **Code Availability:** To enhance reproducibility and facilitate future research, the authors should either provide the code for their algorithms or commit to sharing it publicly upon acceptance of the paper.

**Overall Assessment:**

This paper presents a technically sound and clearly articulated method for low-rank tensor approximation in data streams, leveraging tensor decomposition to generate sketching matrices. The theoretical derivations and the empirical results, which demonstrate improvements over baselines, are notable strengths. However, the main critique concerns the perceived limited novelty, as the algorithms appear to be extensions of existing work rather than fundamentally new paradigms. This, coupled with the absence of code for reproducibility, slightly diminishes the paper's overall impact. Nevertheless, the high clarity, technical quality, and demonstrated performance position this paper marginally above the acceptance threshold. Addressing the minor language issues and committing to code sharing would further strengthen its contribution.

main_points: The proposed algorithm fully exploits data stream structure and obtains quasi-optimal sketching matrices via tensor decomposition on training data.
The method uses a new, non-iterative loss function for sketching matrix computation, which is a relaxation of the IVY tensor decomposition method.
The paper includes theoretical bounds on approximation performance through two theorems.
Empirical analysis on real datasets demonstrates improvements over baselines.
The primary critique is the limited novelty, as the algorithms are perceived as straightforward extensions of existing methods (e.g., SCW).
Reproducibility is a concern due to the absence of code or a commitment to share it.
technical_details: The method is a low-rank tensor approximation algorithm for data streams.
It obtains sketching matrices by performing tensor decomposition on training data.
The loss function used for optimization is a relaxation of that in the IVY tensor decomposition method.
The minimization of the loss function through tensor decomposition is non-iterative.
The algorithms are considered extensions of existing algorithms like SCW.

reviews.summary_of_the_paper: The paper studies the learning-based low-rank approximation problem and proposes a new algorithm, which is based on the tucker tensor decomposition. The new optimization problem based on tensor decomposition can be seen as a relaxation of the original LRA problem. The experimental result suggests that the proposed algorithm has a lower error while having a faster training time.
reviews.strength_and_weaknesses:
Strength

1. The paper proposes an algorithm from a new insight, which has not been considered in the literature on the learning-based LRA. The paper also gives a theoretical guarantee.

2. The experimental results are strong and interesting. The proposed algorithm has a lower error while having a faster training time. 

Weakness

0. Note that the template of this paper is actually a little different than the ICLR template.
1. The analysis of the theorem is straightforward. Besides, the Theorem 2 seems only to give an additive error $\epsilon ||A||_F^2$,  I am wondering that would it be possible to get a multiplicative error $\epsilon ||A- A_k||_F^2$, which is the common setting in the literature of the sketching-based LRA.
2. As mentioned in the paper, the new learning-based sketch matrix is a dense matrix, which I think is the main drawback of this paper. The dense sketch matrix seems not to be useful when the size of the data is large. Note that if $S$ is a Count-Sketch matrix, we can compute $SA$ in $nnz(A)$ time. That is one of the reasons that most of the previous works only study the sparse sketching matrix.  As mentioned in the work of IVY19, One applications scenario of the learning-based sketch involves processing streams of data (video, data logs, customer activity etc) by executing the same algorithm on an hourly, daily or weekly basis. Hence in this sense the testing time is more important if the training time is only a little slower as the training process can be done before the new data comes. Also, notice that the sparse matrix has the pass-efficiency property when the data size exceeds memory available in RAM, which is what the author mentioned in the conclusion section. The update time for one update of the Count-Sketch matrix is $O(1)$.

3. The paper mentions the testing time in the experiment section but it seems not to be done in the correct way. It says in the paper that "have no idea how to accelerate the matrix multiplication SA while exploiting the sparse structure of S". However, suppose that the position and value vectors are $p$, $v$. Note that if the $t$-th non-zero entries of $A$ is $A_{ij} = c$, we just need to do the update 
$
SA[p_i, j] = SA[p_i, j]  + v_i \cdot c . 
$
which means the time to compute $SA$ is the same as to read the matrix $A$. 

4. The authors mention the memory usage in the experiment section. Can the author give a detailed result of the memory usage for all approaches?
