reviews.summary_of_the_paper:
The authors propose learning disentangled representation via regularizing Gaussian Channel Autoencoder (GCAE) with Excess Entropy Power (EEP) loss. The authors claim their proposed method can achieve more reliable and high-performing disentanglement.
Their methods are largely motivated by the curse of dimensionality difficulty issue of the FactorVAE, one of the leading VAE-based disentangling approaches.  FactorVAE modifies beta-VAE objective by specifically penalizing the dependencies between the latent dimensions towards benefiting the Total Correlation (TC). The authors (of this paper) propose to use Dual Total Correlation (DTC) towards avoiding the curse of dimensionality.

The authors examined their proposed approaches on two widely used datasets using one metric – Mutual Information Gap (MIG). Experiments show that their approaches achieve (in average) higher MIG compared with other three leading VAE-based disentanglement approaches.
reviews.strength_and_weaknesses:
The strengths include but not limited to:

-- The proposed methods are well-motivated. The authors performed preliminary experiments to show the effect of curse of dimensionality for non-parametric density estimation, which is illustrated in Figure one. Based on this fact, the authors analyze the TC and DTC, and show the merits of DTC.

-- The proposed method does achieve higher MIG compared with a few leading VAE-based approaches.


The weaknesses include:

-- There are a lot of quantitative and qualitative ways for evaluating disentangled representations. For quantitative evaluation, there are a few widely used metrics besides MIG, including explicitness (Ridgeway & Mozer, 2018), beta score (Higgins et al., 2017), SAP (Kumar et al., 2018) and DCI disentanglement (Eastwood & Williams 2018).  Typically, previous works (on VAE-based disentanglement) would try to show their methods are consistently better using multiple metrics.

-- Besides the three leading approaches (by 2018) mentioned by the authors, there are also some important works missed by the authors. For example, DIP-VAE (Kumar et al., 2017) and CCI-VAE (Burgress et al., 2017). More importantly, latest works on VAE-based disentangled representation learning has already realized the importance of using inductive biases. A recent work published in ICML (Mita et al., 2021) employs a particular form of factorized prior (also conditionally depends on auxiliary variables) towards learning an identifiable model with theoretical guarantees on disentanglement. Te authors could try to compare with some more (especially recent ones) important works using more datasets.

-- The preliminary experiments do show that higher m may cause difficulty in training/optimization for other works (especially Factor VAE), but the whole set of experiments in the paper are done under a single dimension m=10. It would be interesting to conduct more ablation studies to show the benefits of the proposed methods in terms of curse of dimensionality compared with FactorVAE and Beta VAE.

-- Some comments on ablation studies: In Figure 3, Figure 4 and Figure 5, the best performance is achieved using sigma=0.3 (at least for Beamsythesis), did you further try sigma larger than 0.3? Also, in page 5, you mentioned the importance of adding Gaussian noise – it ensures that p(z) is continuous and finite with respect to uniform density. Did you try the scenario where sigma=0?
reviews.clarity_quality_novelty_and_reproducibil:
Overall, the paper’s idea is interesting, while descriptions can be less clear at places

— In the bottom of page 4, the authors wrote ‘to extract a compressed, noise-resistant representation’. Can you elaborate why the representation is noise-resistant?

— Figure two and page 4 descriptions are not aligned. For example, in the figure 2, the encoder with parameter phi (lower case) would generate representation z_phi; In the main text description, you are using uppercase phi. More importantly, u is missing in Figure 2, and the figure 2 is not so informative. 

—It’s unclear to me if m<<n (in page 4) is always realistic.

--Figure 3 caption is missing.
reviews.summary_of_the_review: Overall, the method is novel and demonstrates its effectiveness on learning disentangled representations. However, I do have some concerns/comments as mentioned in my review. I’m slightly not inclined at this moment, but am willing to discuss with authors and change my views accordingly.
reviews.confidence: 3: You are fairly confident in your assessment. It is possible that you did not understand some parts of the submission or that you are unfamiliar with some pieces of related work. Math/other details were not carefully checked.
reviews.full_review_text:
Summary Of The Paper:
The authors propose learning disentangled representation via regularizing Gaussian Channel Autoencoder (GCAE) with Excess Entropy Power (EEP) loss. The authors claim their proposed method can achieve more reliable and high-performing disentanglement.
Their methods are largely motivated by the curse of dimensionality difficulty issue of the FactorVAE, one of the leading VAE-based disentangling approaches.  FactorVAE modifies beta-VAE objective by specifically penalizing the dependencies between the latent dimensions towards benefiting the Total Correlation (TC). The authors (of this paper) propose to use Dual Total Correlation (DTC) towards avoiding the curse of dimensionality.

The authors examined their proposed approaches on two widely used datasets using one metric – Mutual Information Gap (MIG). Experiments show that their approaches achieve (in average) higher MIG compared with other three leading VAE-based disentanglement approaches.

Strength And Weaknesses:
The strengths include but not limited to:

-- The proposed methods are well-motivated. The authors performed preliminary experiments to show the effect of curse of dimensionality for non-parametric density estimation, which is illustrated in Figure one. Based on this fact, the authors analyze the TC and DTC, and show the merits of DTC.

-- The proposed method does achieve higher MIG compared with a few leading VAE-based approaches.


The weaknesses include:

-- There are a lot of quantitative and qualitative ways for evaluating disentangled representations. For quantitative evaluation, there are a few widely used metrics besides MIG, including explicitness (Ridgeway & Mozer, 2018), beta score (Higgins et al., 2017), SAP (Kumar et al., 2018) and DCI disentanglement (Eastwood & Williams 2018).  Typically, previous works (on VAE-based disentanglement) would try to show their methods are consistently better using multiple metrics.

-- Besides the three leading approaches (by 2018) mentioned by the authors, there are also some important works missed by the authors. For example, DIP-VAE (Kumar et al., 2017) and CCI-VAE (Burgress et al., 2017). More importantly, latest works on VAE-based disentangled representation learning has already realized the importance of using inductive biases. A recent work published in ICML (Mita et al., 2021) employs a particular form of factorized prior (also conditionally depends on auxiliary variables) towards learning an identifiable model with theoretical guarantees on disentanglement. Te authors could try to compare with some more (especially recent ones) important works using more datasets.

-- The preliminary experiments do show that higher m may cause difficulty in training/optimization for other works (especially Factor VAE), but the whole set of experiments in the paper are done under a single dimension m=10. It would be interesting to conduct more ablation studies to show the benefits of the proposed methods in terms of curse of dimensionality compared with FactorVAE and Beta VAE.

-- Some comments on ablation studies: In Figure 3, Figure 4 and Figure 5, the best performance is achieved using sigma=0.3 (at least for Beamsythesis), did you further try sigma larger than 0.3? Also, in page 5, you mentioned the importance of adding Gaussian noise – it ensures that p(z) is continuous and finite with respect to uniform density. Did you try the scenario where sigma=0?

Clarity Quality Novelty And Reproducibility:
Overall, the paper’s idea is interesting, while descriptions can be less clear at places

— In the bottom of page 4, the authors wrote ‘to extract a compressed, noise-resistant representation’. Can you elaborate why the representation is noise-resistant?

— Figure two and page 4 descriptions are not aligned. For example, in the figure 2, the encoder with parameter phi (lower case) would generate representation z_phi; In the main text description, you are using uppercase phi. More importantly, u is missing in Figure 2, and the figure 2 is not so informative. 

—It’s unclear to me if m<<n (in page 4) is always realistic.

--Figure 3 caption is missing.

Summary Of The Review:
Overall, the method is novel and demonstrates its effectiveness on learning disentangled representations. However, I do have some concerns/comments as mentioned in my review. I’m slightly not inclined at this moment, but am willing to discuss with authors and change my views accordingly.

Rating:
5: marginally below the acceptance threshold
reviews.text:
## Peer Review

**Overall Assessment:**

This paper introduces the Gaussian Channel Autoencoder (GCAE), a novel method for learning disentangled representations. The core motivation, addressing the curse of dimensionality in density estimation for disentanglement using Dual Total Correlation (DTC) and Excess Entropy Power (EEP) loss, is well-articulated and provides a strong foundation for the proposed approach. Preliminary experiments effectively illustrate the challenges of non-parametric density estimation for Total Correlation (TC) based methods, clearly demonstrating the merits of DTC. The GCAE method itself is novel and shows promising initial results, particularly achieving higher average Mutual Information Gap (MIG) scores compared to several existing VAE-based approaches, indicating its effectiveness.

However, despite these strengths, the current submission has several significant shortcomings that prevent it from being accepted in its present form. These primarily concern the comprehensiveness of the experimental evaluation, the depth of comparison with existing literature, and the clarity of certain descriptions and figures. Addressing these points would substantially strengthen the paper and better validate its contributions.

**Strengths:**

1.  **Strong Motivation:** The paper is well-motivated by the practical challenges of the curse of dimensionality in density estimation, especially when applying TC-based methods for disentanglement. The shift towards DTC is a sound theoretical choice in this context.
2.  **Clear Illustration of Problem:** Figure 1 effectively illustrates the curse of dimensionality in non-parametric density estimation, providing a clear visual foundation for the problem GCAE aims to solve.
3.  **Novelty and Promising Results:** The proposed GCAE method is novel and demonstrates its effectiveness by achieving higher average MIG scores on the evaluated datasets (Beamsynthesis and dSprites) compared to several leading VAE-based disentanglement approaches. It shows potential in learning disentangled representations.

**Weaknesses:**

1.  **Limited Evaluation Metrics:** The quantitative evaluation of disentangled representations is overly reliant on the Mutual Information Gap (MIG) metric. A comprehensive assessment requires including other widely used disentanglement metrics such as explicitness, beta score, SAP, and DCI disentanglement to provide a more holistic view of GCAE's performance across different aspects of disentanglement.
2.  **Incomplete Comparison with Related Works:** The paper overlooks several important related works and baselines in VAE-based disentangled representation learning. Specifically, methods like DIP-VAE, CCI-VAE, and more recent approaches that incorporate inductive biases and offer theoretical guarantees (e.g., Mita et al., 2021) should be included for a more thorough comparison against the state of the art.
3.  **Insufficient Ablation Studies on Latent Space Dimensionality ('m'):** While the paper's motivation revolves around avoiding the curse of dimensionality, most experiments are conducted with a fixed latent space dimensionality of m=10. This limits the demonstration of GCAE's benefits, especially compared to methods like FactorVAE and Beta VAE which struggle with higher 'm'. More extensive ablation studies across varying 'm' are crucial.
4.  **Incomplete Ablation Studies on Gaussian Noise Hyperparameter ('sigma'):** The ablation studies for the Gaussian noise hyperparameter 'sigma' are incomplete. The current exploration does not include values larger than 0.3 or, critically, the scenario where sigma=0, which would serve as an important baseline to understand the contribution of the Gaussian noise.
5.  **Clarity Issues and Inconsistencies:**
    *   The overall clarity of descriptions in the paper is sometimes lacking, hindering full understanding of certain aspects.
    *   The paper does not adequately elaborate on why the representation generated by the GCAE is considered "noise-resistant."
    *   Figure 2, which depicts the GCAE architecture, contains inconsistencies with the main text (e.g., encoder parameter notation), is missing the 'u' variable, and is generally not very informative.
    *   The realism of the assumption 'm << n' (page 4) is questioned and requires further elaboration or justification.
    *   Figure 3 is missing a caption.

**Suggestions for Improvement:**

1.  **Expand Quantitative Evaluation:** Include a broader set of widely used disentanglement metrics (e.g., explicitness, beta score, SAP, DCI disentanglement) to provide a more comprehensive and robust evaluation.
2.  **Broaden Comparisons:** Compare GCAE against a wider range of important and recent related works, especially those focusing on VAE-based disentangled representation learning with inductive biases and theoretical guarantees (e.g., DIP-VAE, CCI-VAE, Mita et al., 2021).
3.  **Extensive Ablation on 'm':** Conduct more extensive ablation studies on the latent space dimensionality 'm'. This should clearly demonstrate GCAE's benefits in avoiding the curse of dimensionality, particularly in comparison to how FactorVAE and Beta VAE perform under higher 'm' values.
4.  **Complete Ablation on 'sigma':** Perform further ablation studies on the 'sigma' hyperparameter, including testing values larger than 0.3 and, importantly, the case where 'sigma=0' to isolate the effect of the noise.
5.  **Enhance Clarity:**
    *   Provide a detailed explanation for why the learned representation is considered 'noise-resistant'.
    *   Ensure consistency between Figure 2 and the main text descriptions (e.g., consistent notation for encoder parameters) and include the 'u' variable in Figure 2. Enhance its overall informativeness.
    *   Clarify the conditions or contexts under which the assumption 'm << n' (page 4) is realistic and justified for the proposed method.
    *   Add the missing caption for Figure 3.

**Recommendation:**

The paper presents a novel and well-motivated approach to disentangled representation learning with promising initial results. The core idea of leveraging DTC and EEP loss to address the curse of dimensionality is compelling. However, the current experimental validation is not sufficiently comprehensive, lacking diverse metrics and comparisons with a broader set of relevant and recent baselines. Furthermore, the ablation studies for key hyperparameters are limited, and several clarity issues exist in the paper's descriptions and figures. These weaknesses significantly hinder the full appreciation and validation of the proposed method's contributions. Therefore, I recommend **marginally below the acceptance threshold**. Substantial revisions addressing the aforementioned weaknesses, particularly in experimental rigor and clarity, would be required to bring this paper to an acceptable standard.

title: Evaluation of Active Feature Acquisition Methods under Missing Data

reviews.summary_of_the_paper: The authors consider the task of active feature acquisition, i.e., the requirement of an actor to choose whether and if so which features to acquire during run time, in the context of missing data during the training stage (in all three variants of missingness). Their proposal formulates the task as a causal graph and formulates it as a reinforcement learning problem. Their final estimator of the true cost function is then evaluated in several experimental settings.
reviews.strength_and_weaknesses:
The authors tackle an important task with a novel and principled approach.  

One weakness of the paper is in the theoretical section 3. The diversity yet similarity of the notation requires close attention to follow. The authors provide a very helpful glossary in the appendix, however, it is never mentioned in the main text. Adding a reference there would allow for greater readability. The second weakness similarly relates to the structure of the paper. Given the length of Section 3, almost all, especially all experiments with real-world data, are hidden in the appendix.
reviews.clarity_quality_novelty_and_reproducibil: The quality of the writing is high, the same goes for the clarity apart from the points mentioned above. The approach itself is novel as far as I can say due to a somewhat limited overview of the literature in that domain. Some training details seem to be missing in the appendix to allow for full reproducibility (e.g., which gradient descent method, activation functions in the neural nets,...).

reviews.full_review_text:
Summary Of The Paper:
The authors consider the task of active feature acquisition, i.e., the requirement of an actor to choose whether and if so which features to acquire during run time, in the context of missing data during the training stage (in all three variants of missingness). Their proposal formulates the task as a causal graph and formulates it as a reinforcement learning problem. Their final estimator of the true cost function is then evaluated in several experimental settings.

Strength And Weaknesses:
The authors tackle an important task with a novel and principled approach.  

One weakness of the paper is in the theoretical section 3. The diversity yet similarity of the notation requires close attention to follow. The authors provide a very helpful glossary in the appendix, however, it is never mentioned in the main text. Adding a reference there would allow for greater readability. The second weakness similarly relates to the structure of the paper. Given the length of Section 3, almost all, especially all experiments with real-world data, are hidden in the appendix.

Clarity Quality Novelty And Reproducibility:
The quality of the writing is high, the same goes for the clarity apart from the points mentioned above. The approach itself is novel as far as I can say due to a somewhat limited overview of the literature in that domain. Some training details seem to be missing in the appendix to allow for full reproducibility (e.g., which gradient descent method, activation functions in the neural nets,...).

Summary Of The Review:
A paper tackling an important task with a principled solution, whose weaknesses lie primarily in its structural presentation and less in its theoretical contribution.

Rating:
8: accept, good paper
reviews.text:
## Peer Review

This paper addresses the important and challenging problem of active feature acquisition (AFA) when faced with missing data (including MCAR, MAR, and MNAR mechanisms) during the training phase. The authors propose a novel and principled approach that formulates the AFA task within the frameworks of causal graphs and reinforcement learning. A new estimator for the true cost function is presented and thoroughly evaluated in experimental settings.

**Strengths:**

The core strength of this work lies in its innovative and principled methodology. The authors' formulation of AFA as both a causal graph problem and a reinforcement learning task is genuinely novel and represents a significant theoretical contribution to the field. This approach is highly relevant and tackles an important practical problem with significant implications. Furthermore, the paper is exceptionally well-written, exhibiting high clarity and quality of exposition, which greatly aids in understanding the complex theoretical underpinnings.

**Weaknesses:**

While the theoretical contribution is strong, there are a few areas primarily related to presentation and reproducibility that could be improved. Firstly, the notation in theoretical Section 3, while precise, can be quite diverse and at times similar, requiring very close attention from the reader to distinguish between various concepts. Secondly, a very helpful glossary is provided in the appendix, but it is not explicitly referenced in the main text, which could significantly enhance readability for readers navigating the complex notation. Thirdly, a structural concern is that a substantial portion of the experimental results, particularly those involving real-world datasets, are largely relegated to the appendix. Given the extensive length of Section 3, integrating more key experimental findings into the main body would improve the paper's flow and impact. Lastly, some training details crucial for full reproducibility are currently missing from the appendix, such as the specific gradient descent method used and the activation functions employed in the neural networks. It is important to emphasize that these weaknesses are primarily structural and presentation-focused, rather than fundamental flaws in the theoretical contribution itself.

**Suggestions for Improvement:**

1.  To enhance readability and user experience, I strongly recommend adding a clear reference to the glossary provided in the appendix within the main text, perhaps early in Section 3 or where new notation is introduced.
2.  For full reproducibility, please include the missing training details in the appendix. Specifically, information regarding the chosen gradient descent method and the activation functions used in any neural network architectures would be highly beneficial.

**Overall Assessment:**

This is a very strong paper that proposes a novel, principled, and important solution to the active feature acquisition problem under missing data. The theoretical foundation is robust, and the writing quality is high. While there are some minor structural presentation issues and a few missing details regarding reproducibility, these are easily addressable and do not detract from the fundamental value and novelty of the work. The paper makes a significant contribution to the field.

**Recommendation:**

**8: accept, good paper**
reviews.original_text:
Summary Of The Paper:
The authors consider the task of active feature acquisition, i.e., the requirement of an actor to choose whether and if so which features to acquire during run time, in the context of missing data during the training stage (in all three variants of missingness). Their proposal formulates the task as a causal graph and formulates it as a reinforcement learning problem. Their final estimator of the true cost function is then evaluated in several experimental settings.

Strength And Weaknesses:
The authors tackle an important task with a novel and principled approach.  

One weakness of the paper is in the theoretical section 3. The diversity yet similarity of the notation requires close attention to follow. The authors provide a very helpful glossary in the appendix, however, it is never mentioned in the main text. Adding a reference there would allow for greater readability. The second weakness similarly relates to the structure of the paper. Given the length of Section 3, almost all, especially all experiments with real-world data, are hidden in the appendix.

Clarity Quality Novelty And Reproducibility:
The quality of the writing is high, the same goes for the clarity apart from the points mentioned above. The approach itself is novel as far as I can say due to a somewhat limited overview of the literature in that domain. Some training details seem to be missing in the appendix to allow for full reproducibility (e.g., which gradient descent method, activation functions in the neural nets,...).

Summary Of The Review:
A paper tackling an important task with a principled solution, whose weaknesses lie primarily in its structural presentation and less in its theoretical contribution.

Rating:
8: accept, good paper

reviews.full_review_text:
Summary Of The Paper:
The paper introduces AFAPE, the first approach active feature acquisition performance evaluation under missing data. It also introduces AFAIS (i.e.,  active feature acquisition importance sampling) a novel estimator that is more efficient than existing approaches.

Strength And Weaknesses:
The paper introduces a novel approach to an important, real-world problem: active feature acquisition. It sets up the problem, provides a novel framework for solving it, and validates it empirically against existing approaches.

However, the paper could be significantly improved by 
(i) a better use of the available space: in its current form, most experimental results (including all on the real-world datasets) are in the appendix. The authors should shrink the current "section 3" by at least two pages (from 5 to at most 3) and use the freed space to bring forward the experiments in the appendix and to thoroughly discuss insights on their significance and weaknesses.
(ii) the paper would greatly benefit from a motivating, real-world dataset. The three datasets in Appendix A-10 appear to be real-world, but the experimental setup is quite vague and confusing (did the author(s) chose arbitrary are the levels of missingness and miss-classification costs? if not, please explain).

Clarity Quality Novelty And Reproducibility:
The paper appears to introduce a novel, first-of-its-kind approach to active feature acquisition. Unfortunately, the paper is not written with a general audience in mind, which limits its potential impact across other communities. Investing half a page on intuitively introducing all key AFA concepts would go a long way to address this issue. Also the paper over-emphasizes the method's detail (5 pages) and, within its main body, it barely touches on the empirical validation (one page on a single, synthetic domain; per the comments above, the experiments from A.10 should be brought within the main paper). The paper would also greatly benefit (in both clarity and significance) if it had an in-depth discussion of the empirical insights, with their strengths and weaknesses).

Summary Of The Review:
Overall, the proposed approach has clear potential, but the paper needs quite a bit of re-writing/re-organizing in order to clarify its impact. As most of the required information is already within the appendices, the comments in this review should quite easy and fast to address.

Rating:
6: marginally above the acceptance threshold
reviews.text:
## Peer Review Report

**Paper Summary:**
This paper introduces AFAPE (Active Feature Acquisition Performance Evaluation), a novel framework designed to evaluate the performance of active feature acquisition methods in scenarios with missing data. It further proposes AFAIS (Active Feature Acquisition Importance Sampling), a new estimator claimed to be more efficient than existing methods for this crucial, real-world problem. The authors validate their approach empirically against current techniques.

**Strengths:**
The paper addresses a highly important and practical problem: the evaluation of active feature acquisition under missing data, presenting a truly novel, first-of-its-kind approach. The problem setup is well-articulated, and the proposed framework (AFAPE) along with the new estimator (AFAIS) offer a promising solution. The empirical validation against existing methods provides initial support for the approach's effectiveness, demonstrating clear potential for future impact.

**Weaknesses:**
While the core idea is strong, the paper suffers from significant organizational and presentation issues that limit its immediate impact and accessibility.
1.  **Inefficient Space Allocation:** There is an over-emphasis on methodological details, with Section 3 (methods) spanning five pages. In contrast, the empirical validation in the main body is constrained to a single page, covering only a synthetic domain. All experimental results pertaining to real-world datasets are unfortunately relegated to the appendix, which makes it challenging for readers to grasp the full scope and practical implications of the work.
2.  **Lack of Clarity for General Audience:** The paper is not written with a general audience in mind, which significantly limits its potential reach across various communities. Key Active Feature Acquisition (AFA) concepts are not intuitively introduced, making the paper difficult to follow for those not already deeply familiar with the field.
3.  **Vague Experimental Setup:** The experimental setup for the real-world datasets, particularly within the appendix, is vague and confusing. Crucial choices, such as the specific levels of missingness and the rationale behind the selected misclassification costs, are not adequately explained.

**Suggestions for Improvement:**
To enhance the paper's clarity, impact, and accessibility, I strongly recommend the following revisions:
1.  **Reorganize Methodological Section:** Shrink Section 3 (methods) by at least two pages, reducing it from five to a maximum of three pages. This can be achieved by streamlining derivations and focusing on core insights.
2.  **Integrate Empirical Results:** Utilize the space freed from Section 3 to bring forward all experimental results, especially those on real-world datasets, from the appendix into the main body of the paper. This will significantly strengthen the empirical validation.
3.  **In-depth Discussion of Empirical Insights:** Provide a thorough and in-depth discussion of the empirical insights derived from the experiments, including their significance, limitations, and potential implications.
4.  **Introduce AFA Concepts:** Dedicate approximately half a page to intuitively introduce all key Active Feature Acquisition (AFA) concepts. This will make the paper much more accessible to a broader audience.
5.  **Clarify Experimental Details:** Explicitly clarify the experimental setup for real-world datasets, providing detailed explanations for the choices made regarding levels of missingness and misclassification costs.
6.  **Overall Re-writing and Re-organization:** Undertake significant re-writing and re-organization across the entire manuscript to improve clarity, flow, and to more effectively highlight the paper's impact.

**Main Points and Overall Assessment:**
The paper introduces a highly novel framework (AFAPE) and estimator (AFAIS) to address the critical, real-world challenge of evaluating active feature acquisition under missing data. This is a significant contribution. However, the primary critique lies in the paper's current organization, which disproportionately emphasizes methodological details while under-representing crucial empirical validation—especially real-world experiments—in the main body. Furthermore, clarity for a general audience is lacking, particularly concerning core AFA concepts and the detailed setup of experiments. Despite these presentation issues, the proposed approach demonstrates clear potential, and many of the necessary improvements can be addressed by reorganizing and clarifying existing content, much of which is already present in the appendices. With substantial revisions addressing the aforementioned points, this work has the potential to be a valuable contribution.

**Decision:**
6: marginally above the acceptance threshold

reviews.summary_of_the_paper:
- The authors tackled an important problem called active feature acquisition (AFA) which is critical but under-explored.
- More specifically, the authors focused on the setting where the training data includes missing components which is also practical in the setting when active feature acquisition is necessary (measurement costs are high).
- The authors do not focus on proposing a new AFA model. Instead, the authors focused on evaluation of AFA models with the training data with missing components.
- The experimental results are promising, showing consistently better evaluation results in comparison to alternatives in both synthetic and real-world datasets.
reviews.strength_and_weaknesses:
Strength:
- The authors tackled an important problem, active feature acquisition. Especially, the authors focused on evaluation of AFA model with missing components which is highly practical problem.
- The proposed solution makes sense and have sample efficiency and generally applicable to any missing patterns. 

Weakness:
- The experimental sections are weak. The datasets are too simple; thus, hard to say that the proposed evaluation method can be well generalized to the real-world datasets.
- In experiments, the authors only focused on one-time AFA. However, in the introduction, the authors mainly discussed about the sequential AFA.
reviews.clarity_quality_novelty_and_reproducibil:
- The paper is easy to read and clear (except the experimental section).
- I think the proposed framework is somewhat novel.
- Some details are missing for the experiments and many details are deferred to the appendix; thus, I cannot provide high score for the reproducibility of this paper.
reviews.summary_of_the_review:
1. Experiments on real-world data
- With real world data we cannot do experiments because the assumption 1 does not hold with logged data.
- So, we should state that the real-world data experiments are actually somewhat semi-synthetic data experiments.

2. IPW
- When we use IPW, one another way is computing the probability of the agent that selects the same subset of logged features.
- Then, use that as the propensity score to compute the unbiased estimator of the cost.
- In other words, we can do weighted average of the logged costs where weights come from the probability that our AFA agent can select the exact subset of logged policy.
- In that case, we do not need to worry about only utilizing the samples with complete cases and this can significantly improve the sample efficiency of the propensity method. 

3. Small number of data points
- In many medical domains, there are only a small number of samples in the datasets.
- It would be great if the authors can provide the results with a small number of samples ranging from 0-5000 as well.

4. Number of features
- It seems like the authors used the synthetic data with 4 features.
- It would be better if the authors can provide the results with more features. 
- Based on the introduction, the authors mentioned emergency care examples with many possible measurements. To make this result more realistic, it would be better to provide the results with at least 20 features.
- Note that all the real-world experiments have less than 10 features.

5. Completeness of the experiments
- As we know, the missing patterns are quite important in this paper.
- In that case, it would be good if the authors provide MCAR, MAR, MNAR settings for one synthetic data and all 3 real-world datasets.
- Currently, the authors only provide one set of experimental results.

reviews.text:
## Peer Review

This paper addresses the critical and underexplored problem of evaluating Active Feature Acquisition (AFA) methods, particularly when training data contains missing components. This is a highly practical and important area, and the proposed solution is both sensible and designed for sample efficiency, exhibiting general applicability across various missing data patterns. The paper is generally well-written and clear, making it accessible to readers, and the proposed framework demonstrates a degree of novelty.

However, the primary limitation of this work lies in its experimental validation, which currently presents significant weaknesses that hinder the generalizability and impact of the proposed evaluation method.

### Strengths

*   **Addresses an Important Problem**: The paper tackles a crucial and practical challenge in machine learning, specifically the evaluation of AFA methods in the presence of missing data, which is often overlooked.
*   **Sensible and Efficient Solution**: The proposed solution is well-conceived, offering a sample-efficient approach that can be applied to any missing data pattern.
*   **Clarity and Readability**: The paper is generally easy to read and clear in its exposition, making the core ideas understandable.
*   **Novelty**: The proposed framework introduces a somewhat novel approach to this evaluation problem.

### Weaknesses

*   **Limited Experimental Validation**: The experimental sections are a major weakness. The datasets used are overly simplistic, which makes it challenging to ascertain the generalizability of the proposed evaluation method to more complex, real-world scenarios.
*   **Mismatch in AFA Focus**: While the introduction extensively discusses sequential AFA, the experimental evaluation is predominantly focused on one-time AFA. This discrepancy creates a disconnect between the problem formulation and the empirical validation.
*   **Reproducibility Concerns**: Key experimental details are often missing from the main paper, with many deferred to the appendix. This significantly hinders the reproducibility of the results and the ability for readers to fully understand the experimental setup.
*   **Questionable Real-World Generalizability**: The "real-world" experiments implicitly rely on Assumption 1, which posits full control of the AFA agent over missingness at run-time. This assumption does not typically hold for logged real-world data, rendering these experiments somewhat semi-synthetic and limiting their true real-world applicability.
*   **Insufficient Scope of Experiments**: The current experiments lack comprehensive coverage across critical dimensions such as varying sample sizes, feature counts, and diverse missingness patterns.

### Suggestions for Improvement

To strengthen the paper and address the identified weaknesses, I recommend the following:

1.  **Clarify Semi-Synthetic Nature**: Explicitly acknowledge and discuss that experiments using real-world logged data are somewhat semi-synthetic due to Assumption 1 (full control over missingness not holding for logged data). This will provide a more transparent context for the findings.
2.  **Enhance IPW Sample Efficiency**: Explore an alternative Inverse Propensity Weighting (IPW) method. Specifically, consider computing the probability of the AFA agent selecting the *exact subset* of logged features and using this as the propensity score for a weighted average of logged costs. This approach could significantly improve sample efficiency by not requiring complete cases.
3.  **Expand Experimental Scope**:
    *   **Small Sample Sizes**: Include experimental results with a small number of samples, specifically ranging from 0 to 5000, as this is highly relevant for many medical domains and other data-scarce applications.
    *   **Higher Feature Count**: Provide experimental results with a larger number of features (at least 20) to make the findings more realistic, especially given the emergency care examples mentioned in the introduction.
    *   **Comprehensive Missingness Patterns**: Present comprehensive experimental results covering MCAR (Missing Completely At Random), MAR (Missing At Random), and MNAR (Missing Not At Random) settings for at least one synthetic dataset and all three real-world datasets, rather than limiting the analysis to a single set of experimental results per dataset.
4.  **Address AFA Type Mismatch**: Either expand the experimental validation to include sequential AFA, aligning with the introduction's discussion, or clearly state in the introduction and experimental sections that the focus is specifically on one-time AFA.
5.  **Improve Reproducibility**: Move critical experimental details from the appendix into the main paper to enhance reproducibility and clarity for the reader.

### Overall Assessment

While the paper tackles an important and underexplored problem with a sensible and somewhat novel solution, the current experimental validation is a significant drawback. The simplicity of the datasets, the limited scope of the experiments, and the issues with generalizability and reproducibility need substantial improvement. However, the core idea is valuable, and the identified weaknesses are addressable. Given the importance of the problem and the foundational strength of the proposed approach, I believe this paper is marginally above the acceptance threshold, provided the authors commit to addressing the experimental shortcomings as outlined in the suggestions.

reviews.summary_of_the_paper: This paper studies the problem of data acquisition at test time when the training data contains missing data. This work tackles the question: which testing policy should be performed to minimize the cost but maximize performance? The paper shows that traditional ways to handle this problem lead to biased results when training data presents non completely at random missingness. The authors propose to an unbiased estimator of the cost associated with a testing policy.
reviews.strength_and_weaknesses:
This paper explores an important problem in machine learning, particularly relevant to healthcare applications. This work provides a clear formalisation of the problem.

However, the model makes a key assumption that weakens the paper: "the order of acquisitions does not matter for the evaluation step". In the medical context, a first test, if positive, might make all other tests unnecessary. So, could further clarify this assumption?
reviews.clarity_quality_novelty_and_reproducibil:
The paper is really clear and the literature gives a good overview of the field (I would recommend including it in the main text). The method is well introduced.

The paper proposes a novel approach to tackle the problem of data acquisition. My only concern is about the assumption of non-sequentiality in the testing procedure.
reviews.summary_of_the_review: This work tackles a central issue in machine learning for healthcare. However, one of the central assumptions weakens the claims of the paper.

reviews.full_review_text:
Summary Of The Paper:
This paper studies the problem of data acquisition at test time when the training data contains missing data. This work tackles the question: which testing policy should be performed to minimize the cost but maximize performance? The paper shows that traditional ways to handle this problem lead to biased results when training data presents non completely at random missingness. The authors propose to an unbiased estimator of the cost associated with a testing policy.

Strength And Weaknesses:
This paper explores an important problem in machine learning, particularly relevant to healthcare applications. This work provides a clear formalisation of the problem.

However, the model makes a key assumption that weakens the paper: "the order of acquisitions does not matter for the evaluation step". In the medical context, a first test, if positive, might make all other tests unnecessary. So, could further clarify this assumption?

Clarity Quality Novelty And Reproducibility:
The paper is really clear and the literature gives a good overview of the field (I would recommend including it in the main text). The method is well introduced.

The paper proposes a novel approach to tackle the problem of data acquisition. My only concern is about the assumption of non-sequentiality in the testing procedure.

Summary Of The Review:
This work tackles a central issue in machine learning for healthcare. However, one of the central assumptions weakens the claims of the paper.

Rating:
6: marginally above the acceptance threshold
reviews.text:
## Peer Review

This paper addresses the critical problem of Active Feature Acquisition Performance Evaluation (AFAPE) in scenarios where training data contains missing values, specifically focusing on non-completely at random (non-MCAR) missingness. The authors aim to determine optimal testing policies that minimize cost while maximizing performance. They effectively demonstrate that traditional evaluation methods can yield biased results under these conditions and propose a novel, unbiased estimator for the cost associated with a given testing policy.

Overall, the paper tackles an important and highly relevant problem, particularly within machine learning for healthcare applications. The problem formalization is clear, and the method is well-introduced, contributing to the paper's overall clarity and readability. The literature review, while currently in the appendix, provides a good overview of the field and context for the proposed approach, which is itself novel in its approach to tackling data acquisition under non-MCAR training data.

However, a central assumption made in the paper significantly weakens some of its claims and implications: the premise that "the order of acquisitions does not matter for the evaluation step," implying non-sequentiality in the testing procedure. This assumption is particularly problematic in many real-world contexts, especially within medicine, where the result of an initial diagnostic test can fundamentally alter the necessity or sequence of subsequent tests. For instance, a positive test for a specific condition might render further, more invasive tests redundant or change their priority. The current model's reliance on this non-sequentiality assumption is a major concern and limits the direct applicability of the proposed unbiased estimator in such scenarios.

**Suggestions for Improvement:**

1.  **Clarify the Non-Sequentiality Assumption:** It is crucial for the authors to further clarify and rigorously discuss the implications of the assumption that "the order of acquisitions does not matter for the evaluation step." If this assumption is a limitation, it needs to be explicitly acknowledged and discussed in the main text, potentially outlining scenarios where it holds true and where it breaks down. Exploring avenues for future work that relax this assumption would also be beneficial.
2.  **Integrate Literature Review:** The comprehensive literature review, which currently resides in the appendix, should be moved to the main text. This would enhance the context and background for readers and strengthen the paper's foundational arguments.

**Overall Assessment:**

This paper introduces a valuable and novel approach to an important problem in active feature acquisition under missing data, with clear relevance to critical applications like healthcare. The proposed unbiased estimator is a significant contribution. While the central assumption regarding non-sequentiality is a notable limitation that needs more thorough discussion and clarification, the strengths of the paper—its clear problem formulation, novel method, and relevance—outweigh this weakness. With the suggested clarifications and structural improvements, this work would be a strong addition to the literature.

**Recommendation:** 6: marginally above the acceptance threshold

reviews.summary_of_the_paper:
This paper tackle the task of evaluating active feature acquisition (AFA) agent when the AFA agent cannot always access the complete set of features during decision making and there is distribution shift between training and evaluation stages. Specifically, it considers a subset of AFA problems where the features acquired by AFA agent is connected to a classification downstream task and the available dataset contains missing entries that could not be acquired. This work mainly tackles non time series AFA problems where the authors consider the task of feature acquisition from a fixed feature set with a sequence view, for which they claim they are the first one to propose such sequence view.

In the methodology part of this paper, the authors first illustrate the difference between set and sequence views through a hospital example where a task-specific missing data graph (m_graph) is plotted for each view. Then the authors present causal graph for AFA problem, where they identify the feature variable X as the confounder because they claim there exits a path $\bar{R} \leftarrow X \rightarrow C$. They also declare that AFA agent is allowed to implement causal interventions on $\bar{R}$ so that the problem of AFA PE can be formulated as a task of evaluating the cost objective $\mathcal{J}$ under causal intervention on $\bar{R}$. To correct the distributional shift for training and testing stages, the authors propose to integrate an existing technique for unbiased estimation, i.e., importance sampling, to their performance evaluation method.  

The authors make strong claim that their method could work on various type of missing observation patterns, including missing completely at random (MCAR), missing at random (MAR), and missing not at random (MNAR). In the main paper, they show empirical results only for MNAR.
reviews.strength_and_weaknesses:
**Strength**

- The problem this paper attempts to tackle is a challenging yet understudied one which is related to many important real-world problem domains such as cost-sensitive diagnosis in healthcare.

**Weaknesses**

[*Scope*] This paper studies the task of evaluating evaluating AFA (Active Feature Acquisition) agents on a very narrow subset of AFA problems where the AFA strategies are defined as reinforcement learning (RL) policies and there is distribution shift between the training and evaluation AFA distributions. I do think the distribution shift for AFA problems is a general challenge existing in both RL-based as well as non RL-based   AFA problems . However, the authors directly connect the AFA problem with off-environment reinforcement learning without claiming the underlying relationship in the two fields, which might mislead the audiences who are new to the AFA literature when reading this paper. Moreover, the main technical solution considered in this work (causal graphs with intervention) is not specifically related to RL. Therefore, only studying performance evaluation (PE) on RL-based AFA problems lacks generality and the work should consider AFAPE with a larger scope.


[*Methodology - Sequence View*]   One major property introduced in the work is about the *sequence view* to select the features, instead of a *set view*.  First, the authors make false claims in several places in the paper, stating the *sequence view* presented in the paper is the *new view on missingness*.  To the best of my knowledge, there are some works from the existing literature studying AFA problems with such *sequence view* already, e.g., [Yin et al, 2020] (cited by the paper). The *sequential view* presented in this work is simply a special case of  the *sequence view* from existing literature. It's unfair to claim the *sequence view* is new when the authors are aware of the work. Second, I do not think the *sequence view* is a better design choice than *set view* for the non-time series AFA problems considered in this paper. For example, when test A and B can be taken in any order, the *sequence view* fail to represent homogeneous property of the sequences $A\rightarrow B$ and $B\rightarrow A$.  Would it be more appropriate to consider an alternative design which combines the *set view* with *sequence view* and let the AFA policy acquire a subset of features at each step in the *sequence view*? 

[*Methodology - Observation Missingness*]  (1)  I suggest the authors to rephrase the term. In conventional AFA literature, people refer *observation missingness* as the natural property for any AFA method, since when employing active learning  by default the algorithm would receive partial observation which comes *observation missingness*. I think the *observation missingness* referred in the paper refers to the missingness between train/evaluation stages which is a different type from that in conventional literature. (2) Though generally *observation missingness* could be classified into missing completely at random, missing at random, and missing not at random, it's suspicious if a performance evaluation (PE) method is able to work perfectly well on all the scenarios. Overall I think it's more possible a PE method could work well on a well defined subrange of AFA problems. 

[*Methodology - RL perspective*] I think the RL problem considered in the paper is offline RL problem, rather than off-policy RL, because the authors describe the task as off-environment policy training. I think the authors claim their work as off-policy RL mostly in the main paper, even though offline RL has been briefly mentioned from one place in appendix. The authors even claim offline RL is data inefficient, which I think might not make sense. I'm concerned if the RL part of its methodology is correct/clear. 

[*Methodology - Causal Graph*] I feel the presented causal graph is too high-level and not significantly novel. It is simplified for some purpose but I expect to see some reasoning over the distribution shift to play around with, such as representing the AFA policy and other factor leading to distributional shift with different variables to derive the formula with. The authors seem to have combined everything leading to *observational missingness* (AFA policy and sources of distributional shift) in one variable $R$, so that it is still unclear what happens to AFA PE model when we deal with the AFA policy and other assumed sources of distributional shift (other than AFA policy) respectively.

[*Evaluation - Choice of baselines*] I feel the choice of  AFA RL policies are insufficient. So far the authors only adopt CATE and DQN, which is insufficient. CATE is an unpublished work and DQN is a weak off-policy baseline. It would be strong if the authors consider state-of-the-art RL methods proposed for AFA problems like SeqVAE. 

[*Evaluation - Results*] Though the paper makes rather strong claim saying the method can tackle various of missing patterns, i.e., missing completely at random, missing at random, and missing not at random, the empirical evidence is quite weak.  (1)  the evaluation results in main paper only consists of the MNAR domain, which is insufficient to support the authors' claim. (2) The authors claim their method could obtain the unbiased estimate with Figure 5, but I do not find so. It seems AFAIS with IPW base results in considerable biases compared with the baseline.  (3) There should be some suggestions on which type of methods fit for wha type of missingness, but the authors only present partial view on MNAR.  


[*Evaluation - Scenarios*] I think it would be good if the authors could claim their method could work well under various types of interventions which could well cover the observation missingness types. In the current setting, it's unclear how sensitive the proposed PE method would be. 

**Other questions**:
- a ML - > an ML
- $\bar{U}^{(k)} = observe(X_i)$: this definition is ambitious as the authors do not introduce the relationship between $i$ and $k$.
reviews.clarity_quality_novelty_and_reproducibil:
**Clarity**

The writing of the paper is unclear and the organization of its content is not that great. The paper is formulation heavy but they are introduces in a unclear/confusing way, e.g., some characters like X and R are used to define various different variables like $X$, $\hat{X}$ and $\bar{X}$, which lack clear explanation and appear to be hard to understand when reading. The organization is not good as well, since many important parts of this work, e.g., literature for AFA methods, math derivations for the method and empirical evaluation results, are presented at appendix. The main paper is not self contained to a great extent and it not fair to let the future audiences to read through the 20+ pages to understand this work.

**Quality**

Overall I feel quality of this paper is limited at the current state because of its developed for solving a small subset of problems in AFAPE, with a very high-level causal graph derivation and weak evaluation result. 

n. Also I feel the derived causal graph is too high-level not problem-specific. I do not see apparent components representing the force of *observation missingness* and the authors seem to combine the AFA policy together with environmental factors leading to the *observation missingness* in one variable $R$. 

**Novelty**

I feel the novelty is very limited, and the authors overclaim the novelty of various important part of the paper. The authors claim they are the first to introduce the sequence view, but there is a referred work that already done so [Yin et al. 2020]. Also, since the proposed technical contribution of this paper is mostly build on the existing well adopted important sampling regime, the novelty of the solution to their highlighted AFAPE problem is also limited. 

**Reproducibility**

The reproducibility of the paper is low. The experiment engages AFA agent trained by two methods, CATE and DQN, implemented on various domains. I do not see sections introducing the important empirical settings, such as hardware/software config, architectural config for the agents, training iterations, etc, on each testified domain if they differ.
reviews.summary_of_the_review: This paper learns an interesting problem of AFAPE, but I have concerns on its writing, problem scope, the capability of tackling all different types of *observational missingness* in one causal graph, the over simplified causal graph and its insufficient empirical evaluation.  There's a possibility the proposed method could work well on a well specified subset of AFA problems with certain property.

Summary Of The Paper:
This paper tackle the task of evaluating active feature acquisition (AFA) agent when the AFA agent cannot always access the complete set of features during decision making and there is distribution shift between training and evaluation stages. Specifically, it considers a subset of AFA problems where the features acquired by AFA agent is connected to a classification downstream task and the available dataset contains missing entries that could not be acquired. In the methodology part of this paper, the authors first illustrate the difference between set and sequence views through a hospital example where a task-specific missing data graph (m_graph) is plotted for each view. Then the authors present causal graph for AFA problem, where they identify the feature variable X as the confounder because they claim there exits a path $\bar{R} \leftarrow X \rightarrow C$. They also declare that AFA agent is allowed to implement causal interventions on $\bar{R}$ so that the problem of AFA PE can be formulated as a task of evaluating the cost objective $\mathcal{J}$ under causal intervention on $\bar{R}$. To correct the distributional shift for training and testing stages, the authors propose to integrate an existing technique for unbiased estimation, i.e., importance sampling, to their performance evaluation method.  

The authors make strong claim that their method could work on various type of missing observation patterns, including missing completely at random (MCAR), missing at random (MAR), and missing not at random (MNAR). In the main paper, they show empirical results only for MNAR.

Strength And Weaknesses:
**Strength**

- The problem this paper attempts to tackle is a challenging yet understudied one which is related to many important real-world problem domains such as cost-sensitive diagnosis in healthcare.

**Weaknesses**

[*Scope*] This paper studies the task of evaluating evaluating AFA (Active Feature Acquisition) agents on a very narrow subset of AFA problems where the AFA strategies are defined as reinforcement learning (RL) policies and there is distribution shift between the training and evaluation AFA distributions. I do think the distribution shift for AFA problems is a general challenge existing in both RL-based as well as non RL-based   AFA problems . However, the authors directly connect the AFA problem with off-environment reinforcement learning without claiming the underlying relationship in the two fields, which might mislead the audiences who are new to the AFA literature when reading this paper. Moreover, the main technical solution considered in this work (causal graphs with intervention) is not specifically related to RL. Therefore, only studying performance evaluation (PE) on RL-based AFA problems lacks generality and the work should consider AFAPE with a larger scope.

The *sequential view* presented in this work is simply a special case of  the *sequence view* from existing literature. It's unfair to claim the *sequence view* is new when the authors are aware of the work. Second, I do not think the *sequence view* is a better design choice than *set view* for the non-time series AFA problems considered in this paper. For example, when test A and B can be taken in any order, the *sequence view* fail to represent homogeneous property of the sequences $A\rightarrow B$ and $B\rightarrow A$.  Would it be more appropriate to consider an alternative design which combines the *set view* with *sequence view* and let the AFA policy acquire a subset of features at each step in the *sequence view*? 

[*Methodology - Observation Missingness*]  (1)  I suggest the authors to rephrase the term. In conventional AFA literature, people refer *observation missingness* as the natural property for any AFA method, since when employing active learning  by default the algorithm would receive partial observation which comes *observation missingness*. I think the *observation missingness* referred in the paper refers to the missingness between train/evaluation stages which is a different type from that in conventional literature. (2) Though generally *observation missingness* could be classified into missing completely at random, missing at random, and missing not at random, it's suspicious if a performance evaluation (PE) method is able to work perfectly well on all the scenarios. Overall I think it's more possible a PE method could work well on a well defined subrange of AFA problems. 

[*Methodology - RL perspective*] I think the RL problem considered in the paper is offline RL problem, rather than off-policy RL, because the authors describe the task as off-environment policy training. I think the authors claim their work as off-policy RL mostly in the main paper, even though offline RL has been briefly mentioned from one place in appendix. The authors even claim offline RL is data inefficient, which I think might not make sense. I'm concerned if the RL part of its methodology is correct/clear. 

[*Methodology - Causal Graph*] I feel the presented causal graph is too high-level and not significantly novel. It is simplified for some purpose but I expect to see some reasoning over the distribution shift to play around with, such as representing the AFA policy and other factor leading to distributional shift with different variables to derive the formula with. The authors seem to have combined everything leading to *observational missingness* (AFA policy and sources of distributional shift) in one variable $R$, so that it is still unclear what happens to AFA PE model when we deal with the AFA policy and other assumed sources of distributional shift (other than AFA policy) respectively.

[*Evaluation - Choice of baselines*] I feel the choice of  AFA RL policies are insufficient. So far the authors only adopt CATE and DQN, which is insufficient. CATE is an unpublished work and DQN is a weak off-policy baseline. It would be strong if the authors consider state-of-the-art RL methods proposed for AFA problems like SeqVAE. 

[*Evaluation - Results*] Though the paper makes rather strong claim saying the method can tackle various of missing patterns, i.e., missing completely at random, missing at random, and missing not at random, the empirical evidence is quite weak.
